modules.title: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is
modules.kicker: The mental shift before the first keystroke

sourceChapters.title: Intro: From Beginner to AI Native
What Is Claude Code (Chat, Cowork, Code)

modules.plainSummary: Claude Code has the word 'code' in the name, but you do not write code to use it. It is a chat window that can also open, read, and edit the actual files on your computer — your spreadsheets, your scripts, your show notes — and reach out to things online. This module explains the three Claude products (Chat, Cowork, Code), why Claude Code is the powerful one, and the single analogy that makes the whole thing click: the AI model is an engine, Claude Code is the car built around it, and you are the driver. The rest of the module is mindset: the difference between writing clever prompts and giving the AI real context about your station, why your judgment about what is good still decides everything, and why you should think of yourself as a manager of a very fast, very literal new hire.
modules.whyItMatters: Most station staff have only ever used AI as a chatbot in a browser tab — retyping the same background every single time, getting generic output back, and concluding it is not worth the trouble. The shift here is that Claude Code can already know your pledge calendar, your style guide, your last twelve underwriting scripts, and your CPB reporting language, because it can read the folders where those things already live. That is the difference between a smart stranger and a colleague who has been at the station for three years.

concepts.plain: Anthropic makes three things. Chat is the website you already know. Cowork is a simpler tool for everyday office automation. Claude Code is the most powerful, and despite the name it needs no coding.
concepts.stationTranslation: Chat is asking a smart temp a question. Claude Code is that temp with a desk in your building, a key to the file room, and your style guide already read.

concepts.plain: The AI model (Opus, Sonnet, Haiku) is the engine. Claude Code is the car built around it — steering, doors, trunk. You are the driver. Swap engines or swap cars; the driver is the constant.
concepts.stationTranslation: The model is the transmitter. Claude Code is the studio and console around it. You are still the one deciding what actually goes on the air.

concepts.plain: The real difference: Claude Code works inside a folder you point it at. It can open, read, and change the files there — and still search the web when it needs to.
concepts.stationTranslation: Point it at your 'Fall Drive 2026' folder and it can read last year's scripts, the goal sheet, and the copy that flopped — instead of you re-explaining all of it.

concepts.plain: Prompts are how you ask. Context is what the AI actually knows. As models improve, clever prompt wording matters less — what it knows about your world matters more, and always will.
concepts.stationTranslation: A new intern is not useless because you phrased the assignment badly. They are useless until someone tells them what the station does, who's on the desk, and what's on the air tonight.

concepts.plain: As output gets good, the temptation is to stop reading it. The skill that survives is knowing what good looks like — and correcting the system every time it misses, so it misses less.
concepts.stationTranslation: AI can draft the newscast tease. You still know whether it sounds like your station or like a press release. Your name is on the air either way.

concepts.term: Iteration speed and a definition of done
concepts.plain: You almost never get it right in one shot. Build the ugly version fast, see what breaks, feed the correction back. And decide up front what 'done' means, or you will tinker forever.
concepts.stationTranslation: Like teaching a kid to ride a bike: hand on the back, correct the lean, let go a little more each run. Pick one number — hours saved, spots logged — and stop when you hit it.

concepts.plain: A plain workflow is a vending machine: same input, same output, cheap, never surprises you. An AI agent is a slot machine: powerful when the input is messy and reasoning is needed, but variable, pricier, and it fails in odd ways.
concepts.stationTranslation: Emailing the traffic log every Monday needs no AI — that's a vending machine. Reading 200 messy listener emails and sorting what they actually want does.

mindsetShifts.plain: It's relative, not absolute. You don't need to be an expert — you need to know more than the people around you, and to have actually shipped something with it.
mindsetShifts.stationTake: You do not need to out-nerd Silicon Valley. You need to be the person at your station who has actually cut a four-hour task to forty minutes and can show it in a staff meeting.

mindsetShifts.shift: Taste and judgment are the skill that survives
mindsetShifts.plain: Anyone can generate. The scarce skill is knowing what's good, catching the tells, and refusing to ship what isn't.
mindsetShifts.stationTake: Editorial judgment is the whole job and always was. AI does not lower that bar — it raises the volume of things you now have to hold to it.

mindsetShifts.shift: Become a context engineer, not a prompt engineer
mindsetShifts.plain: Prompts are how you ask; context is what it knows. Stop opening blank chats. Give it your real documents, history, and priorities.
mindsetShifts.stationTake: The AI cannot know your pledge script conventions, your music rotation rules, or your CPB reporting language unless you hand it the folder where those live.

mindsetShifts.plain: Build the ugly version fast, see what breaks, correct it, repeat. Every pass is data. Also: know when to stop — define 'done' before you start.
mindsetShifts.stationTake: Ship the rough draft of the automation on a Tuesday, not the perfect one next quarter. Tie it to one number — hours off the log, minutes off show prep — and stop there.

mindsetShifts.shift: Build your Jarvis — but default to the simplest thing
mindsetShifts.plain: The goal is systems that run without you triggering them. But the moment you leave the loop, risk goes up — and many tasks need a plain workflow, not an AI agent at all.
mindsetShifts.stationTake: The impressive move is not an AI agent for everything. It's being the person who says 'this one is just a scheduled script — cheaper, safer, and it will never hallucinate a donor's name.'

mindsetShifts.shift: Unemployment insurance — one passion, many branches
mindsetShifts.plain: Build more than one income stream from the same expertise rather than five unrelated ones. Building in public is the default way to become discoverable.
mindsetShifts.stationTake: The transcript's least station-relevant point, and worth naming as such: public media staff have contracts, outside-activity policies, and a nonprofit's conflict-of-interest rules. Read them before acting on this one.

mindsetShifts.shift: AI native is not what you know — it's what your hand reaches for
mindsetShifts.plain: The test isn't how many models you can name. It's whether your reflex, when a task lands, is to open the AI or to open the same browser tab you always have.
mindsetShifts.stationTake: When a records request or a 90-minute meeting recording lands, does your hand go to the transcript tool by reflex — or do you still start typing notes by hand?

mindsetShifts.shift: Don't quit in the dip — the payoff is the climb
mindsetShifts.plain: Learning a new method costs you speed for a week or two. Most people quit inside that dip, right before the returns turn exponential.
mindsetShifts.stationTake: Week one you will feel slower than you were with your old process. That is the dip, not the verdict. Budget for it before you tell the GM whether it worked.

mindsetShifts.shift: You are just a manager of AI agents
mindsetShifts.plain: Onboard it, tell it clearly what good and bad look like, review what it hands back, give feedback, and update its instructions so next time is better. All of it in plain English.
mindsetShifts.stationTake: If you have ever supervised a summer intern in the newsroom, you already have the entire skill. You just have to keep actually reading their work.

modules.mindsetShiftsNote: Accuracy note: the video's chapter is titled 'The 12 Mindset Shifts' and Nate says he wrote a book around 12 of them, but the transcript never enumerates all twelve. What he actually delivers is a six-skill list (items 1-6 above), then he returns and names three shifts explicitly (items 7-9), calling one of them 'number four' before moving on to installation. The nine above are everything the transcript genuinely contains — we did not invent the missing three.

glossary.definition: The tool wrapped around an AI model that gives it hands — file access, web search, memory. Claude Code is a harness.
The raw AI brain — Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, Fable. Interchangeable. Claude Code runs on the same ones as Claude Chat.
Everything the AI actually knows about your situation: your files, your history, your priorities. Not the same as your prompt.
How much the AI can hold in its head at once — like a producer's prep folder for one show. It is finite.
Wording a request well. Was a big deal; matters less each year as models improve on their own.
Filling the AI's working memory with exactly the right information. Karpathy's phrase. The durable skill.
AI that reasons and decides in a loop rather than following fixed steps. Powerful, variable, more expensive, fails in surprising ways.
A fixed if-this-then-that sequence with no AI in it. Predictable, cheap, boring, almost never breaks. Often the right answer.

useCases.title: The news director who has never opened a terminal
useCases.scenario: You get a 400-page county budget PDF the night before a council vote and one reporter who is already filing two other stories.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Drop the PDF in a folder, point Claude Code at it, and ask it to pull every line item that changed by more than 10% year over year, with page numbers. It reads the whole thing in minutes and cites where it found each figure so your reporter can verify against the source. This is the local-files idea from Chapter 1 — the AI works in your folder, not in a browser tab.
useCases.timeSaved: A full evening of skimming becomes ~20 minutes of reading a cited summary
useCases.guardrail: Nothing it produces goes on air or online until your reporter has opened the actual PDF page it cited and confirmed the number. AI finds the lead; a human confirms it. Always.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Save the budget PDF into a folder on your computer — call it something like "budget-story" so you can find it again.
Open Claude Code and point it at that folder (this just means telling it which folder on your computer to work in).
Ask it to read the whole PDF and list every line item where the amount changed by more than 10% since last year.
Ask it to give you the page number in the PDF where it found each one, not just the number itself.
Open the actual PDF to each page it cited and check that the number really says what Claude said it says.
Hand your reporter only the items you've personally confirmed on the page, with the page numbers attached.
runbook.verify: Open the PDF to the page number Claude cited for each flagged line item — if what's on that page doesn't match the number in Claude's list, pull that item before it goes anywhere near a script.

useCases.title: Onboard Claude to your drive the way you'd onboard an intern
useCases.scenario: Every fall drive, you rewrite the same pitch scripts from scratch because last year's live in someone's Google Drive and nobody remembers which ones actually converted.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Put your past scripts, your goal sheet, and your notes on what worked and what flopped into one folder — the summer-intern move from Chapter 4. Now Claude Code drafts against your station's actual voice and history instead of generic best practices. Correct it once, tell it why, and the next draft is closer.
useCases.timeSaved: Two days of script rewriting becomes an afternoon of editing
useCases.guardrail: Scripts and copy only. Never put the donor file in that folder — no names, no addresses, no giving history, no CRM export. The AI needs your language, not your list.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Gather only past pitch scripts, your goal sheet, and notes on what worked or flopped — leave out anything with a donor's name, email, address, or CRM export.
Put those files in one folder on your computer, named something like "drive-context."
Open Claude Code and point it at that folder.
Ask it to read everything and tell you back, in its own words, what your station's pitch voice sounds like and what's worked before.
Ask it to draft this year's pitch scripts in that voice, aimed at the goals on your goal sheet.
Read every draft, mark up what's off, and tell Claude Code why — not just what — you changed it.
Ask for one more pass using your corrections before you call any script final.
runbook.verify: Open the drive-context folder yourself and search it for anything that looks like a donor name, email, or CRM export — if you find one, delete it and start the folder over before Claude Code ever reads it.

useCases.title: Know when the answer is 'this needs no AI at all'
useCases.scenario: Your GM wants 'an AI' to handle underwriting spot copy end to end because a vendor demo looked impressive.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Use the vending-machine-vs-slot-machine test from Chapter 5. Emailing the weekly spot schedule is a vending machine — a plain scheduled script, no AI, never fails. Turning a client's messy intake email into a compliant draft spot is genuinely messy input and needs reasoning, so that part earns the AI. Being the person who can draw that line is the point.
useCases.timeSaved: Avoids a six-week build for something a five-minute script does
useCases.guardrail: FCC rules do not care that a machine wrote it. Every spot still gets human compliance review: no calls to action, no price claims, no qualitative claims. That review never gets automated.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Write down every underwriting task your GM wants "an AI" to handle, one per line.
For each one, ask: does this follow the exact same steps every single time, or does it need someone to read something messy and make a judgment call?
Route the same-every-time tasks — like emailing the weekly spot schedule — to a plain scheduled script. That's not a Claude Code job at all.
For the genuinely messy task — turning a client's rambling intake email into a spot draft — open Claude Code and ask it to draft compliant copy from that email, in your station's usual format.
Tell it plainly, up front, what a spot must never contain: no calls to action, no price claims, no qualitative claims.
Send every draft it produces through your normal FCC compliance review before it reaches a client or the air.
Show your GM the actual split — one boring script, one Claude Code draft with human review — as the real answer to "get an AI on this."
runbook.verify: Read the compliance reviewer's notes on the first three Claude-drafted spots — if a call-to-action or price claim slipped through in any of them, tighten your instructions before you trust the next batch.

useCases.title: Prep the local-music segment without the tab-switching
useCases.scenario: Your local music host spends two hours before every show pulling artist bios, tour dates, and release news across a dozen browser tabs.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: This is the 'what your hand reaches for' shift. Instead of opening the same tabs, hand Claude Code the week's playlist file and let it web-search and assemble a single prep sheet per artist. It reads your local file and reaches out online — both at once, which Chat cannot do.
useCases.timeSaved: A 2-hour prep block becomes ~25 minutes of review
useCases.guardrail: Anything the host says on air about an artist — a tour date, a label, a hometown — gets confirmed against a real source. A hallucinated tour date is your station's error, not the AI's.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Open your week's playlist file — the one your local-music host already has.
Put it in a folder on your computer and open Claude Code there.
Ask Claude Code to read the playlist and, for each local artist, search online for a short bio, upcoming tour dates, and any recent release news.
Ask it to put all of that into one prep sheet, organized by artist, in the order they're playing.
Read the prep sheet and flag anything that sounds off or too good to be true.
Before your host says anything on air about a tour date, label, or hometown, check it against a real source — the artist's own site or a wire story.
runbook.verify: Pick three claims from the prep sheet — a tour date, a label, a hometown — and look each one up yourself on the artist's own site before your host reads any of them on air.

useCases.title: Budget for the dip before you report on it
useCases.scenario: You green-light an AI pilot and someone asks for results after two weeks. The team is slower than before and morale is dropping.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: That is the dip from Chapter 6, and it is expected, not failure — a short-term productivity cost before the exponential return. Set the definition-of-done up front: one team, one workflow, one metric (hours off show prep, days off the LCSR draft), and a realistic window. Then judge it.
useCases.timeSaved: Prevents killing a working pilot three weeks early
useCases.guardrail: Disclose to your board and to staff what you are piloting and where. Public media runs on trust; a quiet AI rollout that surfaces later is a much bigger problem than a slow one announced early.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Before you announce the pilot, pick exactly one team, one workflow, and one number you're measuring — hours off show prep, days off the LCSR draft, whatever's real for that team.
Set a realistic window for judging it — a few weeks, not a few days — and write that window down where the team can see it.
Tell your board and your staff what you're piloting and where, before anyone asks. Public trust runs on this, not surprise announcements.
When week one comes in slower than the old process, name it out loud as "the dip," not a verdict on the pilot.
Keep a simple running log of the one number you picked, every week of the window.
At the end of the window — not before — sit down with the team and the log, and decide, based on that number, whether it worked.
runbook.verify: Look at the log: compare the number from week one to the number from the final week. If you can't put two real numbers side by side, you don't have a verdict — you have a feeling, and a feeling is what would have killed this pilot three weeks early.

tryThis.title: Give Claude Code a first day at your station
tryThis.setup: Once Claude Code is installed (that's the next module), make one folder on your desktop called 'station-context'. Put in only public, non-sensitive things: your style guide, three underwriting scripts you're proud of, last quarter's newsletter, your on-air pledge script. No donor data. Then open Claude Code in that folder.
tryThis.prompt: Read every file in this folder. This is context about the public radio station I work at. Then write me a one-page summary of what you learned, organized as: (1) what this station sounds like — tone, sentence length, what it avoids; (2) three conventions our underwriting copy follows; (3) five things you'd need to know about us that are NOT in these files. For part 3, ask me the questions rather than guessing. Do not write anything for air yet — I just want to know what you actually understand about us.

pitfalls: The 'code' in Claude Code scares people off. There is no coding. It is a chat window that can also open the files on your computer — that is the entire difference from the Claude website.
Opening a blank chat every time and re-explaining your station from scratch. That is the single habit this module exists to break. Give it the folder, not the paragraph.

Trusting output because it reads well. The video's own warning: when the drafts get good, people stop reading them. In public media, an unread AI draft that reaches air or a donor is a trust problem, not a typo.
Reaching for an AI agent when a boring scheduled script would do. Agents are slot machines — variable, pricier, they fail in odd ways. Default to the simplest thing that works.
Quitting in the dip. Week one with a new method feels slower than the old way. That is the expected cost, not the result. Do not report it to your GM as a failure.
The 'stack side income streams' skill translates badly to public media. Check your outside-activity policy and your station's conflict-of-interest rules before you act on it.

keyQuotes.quote: Prompts are how you ask. Context is what your AI actually knows.
AI can generate the work. Taste is deciding what deserves your name.
AI native isn't what you know. It's about what your hand reaches for.

modules.title: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File
modules.kicker: Install it, run it, verify it

sourceChapters.title: Installing Claude Code & Your First Prompts

modules.plainSummary: This is your first thirty minutes. You install Claude Code (the desktop app is the easy path — no terminal required), you sign in with a paid Claude plan, and you point it at a folder on your own computer. From there it can find files, read them, and build new ones — the demo in the video takes a pile of raw YouTube stats and returns a finished, formatted Excel workbook in about ten minutes, written entirely in plain English. You then learn the four things that make a prompt work (role, context, what NOT to do, and proof), and how to make the AI verify its own output instead of you catching every mistake. Finally, the money part: what a token is, and when Haiku, Sonnet, or Opus is worth reaching for.
modules.whyItMatters: Every station already has the file. The pledge-drive results spreadsheet, the music log, the underwriting traffic report, the CPB reporting workbook — they exist, and somebody spends a weekend wrestling each one into a shape a board or a funder can read. This module is the point where that stops being a weekend and starts being a prompt, and where you learn how to prove the numbers are right before anyone sees them.

concepts.plain: Claude Code runs in the Claude desktop app, in a code editor, or in a terminal window. Same engine underneath. Pick whichever looks least scary.
concepts.stationTranslation: Like choosing between the studio board and the remote kit — same signal path, different surface. Start with the desktop app; nothing is lost if you switch later.

concepts.plain: Claude Code can look inside folders on your own machine — find a file you half-remember, read it, edit it, move it, and make new files next to it.
concepts.stationTranslation: It is a producer who can actually open the shared drive. 'Find the Q2 pledge results somewhere in Downloads' is a real, working instruction.

concepts.term: It builds the deliverable, not just the answer
concepts.plain: Ask for analysis and you get a chat reply. Ask for a workbook and you get an actual .xlsx on disk — dashboard tab, per-item scorecard, trends, color-coded.
concepts.stationTranslation: The output is the thing you hand the GM or the board, not notes you then have to retype into a spreadsheet at 11pm.

concepts.term: The four levers of a prompt
concepts.plain: Role (who it is), context and background (what the situation actually is), negative prompting (what NOT to do), and verification (prove it).
concepts.stationTranslation: Same as briefing a new intern before a shift: who you are today, what's going on, what not to touch, and show me before it airs.

concepts.plain: Explicitly say what not to do. A capable but inexperienced helper will try things you never imagined unless you rule them out up front.
concepts.stationTranslation: 'Do not invent a number that isn't in the file. Do not round. Do not include any donor names.' Say it out loud, every time.

concepts.term: Make the AI prove its work
concepts.plain: Tell it how to check itself. Unverified, a first pass lands around 60% right and you grind it up by hand. Self-verifying, it starts near 80% and you finish in two passes.
concepts.stationTranslation: The check the AI runs is the check you would have run: re-add the column, re-count the rows, cross-check the total against the source file. Ask for that check in the prompt.

concepts.plain: You are billed by token — roughly three-quarters of a word. Output tokens cost about five times input. Haiku is fast and cheap, Sonnet is balanced, Opus is the most capable and the most expensive.
concepts.stationTranslation: Haiku for sorting and tidying. Sonnet for most real work. Opus when the reasoning has to be right the first time — grant narrative, board analysis, anything a funder reads.

glossary.definition: The unit AI bills in. Roughly 3/4 of a word. Text going in is input; text coming out is output.
What you feed the model vs what it writes back. Output costs about 5x more per token.
Claude's models: fast-and-cheap, balanced, and most-capable-and-priciest. Same tool, different horsepower.
The 'do NOT do this' part of your instructions. Guardrails, stated before the work starts.
Making the AI check its own output against something real before it hands the work back to you.
On a Claude subscription you get a rolling 5-hour usage window plus a weekly cap, instead of per-token billing.

useCases.title: Turn the pledge-drive export into a board-ready readout
useCases.scenario: The drive ended Sunday. You have a 3,000-row export from the CRM and a board meeting Thursday, and nobody has time to build the pivot tables again.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Strip the export down to non-identifying columns first — gift amount, date, hour, premium, new-vs-renewing, channel — and save that anonymized file locally. Point Claude Code at it and ask for a workbook: dashboard tab, hour-by-hour take, new vs. renewing split, comparison to last drive. Add the verification line so it re-sums every column against the source before handing it over.
useCases.timeSaved: A two-day spreadsheet weekend becomes about 30 minutes plus a review pass
useCases.guardrail: Donor names, emails, addresses, phone numbers and giving history never leave your CRM. Export the numbers, not the people. Strip PII before Claude ever sees the file.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Export only the columns you need from your CRM — gift amount, date, hour, premium, new-vs-renewing, channel. No names, emails, addresses, or phone numbers.
Put that stripped file in a folder on your computer called something like "drive-readout."
Open Claude Code in that folder and ask it to read the file and list back the columns it sees, before it does anything else.
Ask for the workbook you need: a summary tab, an hour-by-hour take, a new-vs-renewing split, and a comparison to your last drive.
Tell it to re-sum every column against the source file and show you that check before handing the workbook back.
Check the workbook's totals against your CRM's own totals for the drive before the numbers go anywhere near the board.
Save the prompt as a Skill, so the station owns this for next drive, not just you.
runbook.verify: Open the finished workbook and search it for an @ sign — if any email address survived the strip, it isn't safe to work with; start over from a clean export.

useCases.title: Read the music log without reading the music log
useCases.scenario: Your automation system spits out a play log every week. You want to know whether local artist rotation actually hit the target you promised in the last grant, and whether new music share is drifting.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Drop the log export in a folder and ask Claude Code to tally spins by artist, flag which are tagged local, chart the local percentage week over week, and write the counts into a spreadsheet you can reuse. Tell it explicitly not to guess an artist's local status when the tag is blank — flag those rows for a human instead.
useCases.timeSaved: A 4-hour manual tally becomes ~20 minutes
useCases.guardrail: Claude counts spins; it does not decide licensing or reporting obligations. Anything headed to SoundExchange, ASCAP or BMI goes through the person who actually owns that filing.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Export this week's play log from your automation system.
Put the file in a folder on your computer and open Claude Code there.
Ask it to read the log, tally spins by artist, and flag which ones are tagged local.
Tell it plainly: if the local tag is blank, don't guess — flag that row for you to decide, don't count it either way.
Ask it to chart the local percentage week over week and write the counts into a spreadsheet you can reuse.
Review the flagged blank-tag rows yourself and fill in the local status by hand.
Compare the resulting local percentage to the number you promised in your last grant, before you report it anywhere.
runbook.verify: Pick five rows at random from the tally and count their spins yourself straight from the log — if your hand count doesn't match what Claude reported for those five, don't trust the rest of the tally either.

useCases.title: Assemble the CPB Local Content & Service Report evidence pile
useCases.scenario: The LCSR is due and the underlying numbers are scattered across five spreadsheets, three departments and one person's Downloads folder.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Ask Claude Code to find the relevant files across your project folder, read them, and build a single consolidated worksheet with every figure traced back to its source file and cell. Then ask it to prove the work: recompute each total from the raw file and show its arithmetic next to the number it reported.
useCases.timeSaved: A 6-hour hunt-and-reconcile pass becomes ~45 minutes
useCases.guardrail: Claude assembles and cross-checks; a human signs the report. Never let it fill a gap by estimating — an empty cell stays empty and gets escalated.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Gather the five spreadsheets — wherever they actually live, including that one person's Downloads folder — into a single folder on your computer.
Open Claude Code and point it at that folder.
Ask it to find every figure the LCSR needs across those files and build one worksheet, with each number traced back to its source file and cell.
Tell it plainly: if a number isn't in any of the files, leave the cell empty and flag it for you — never estimate or guess a fill-in.
Ask it to recompute each total straight from the raw file and show its arithmetic next to the number it reported.
Chase down the real number yourself for every flagged empty cell before the report goes anywhere.
Have the person who owns the LCSR filing review and sign the consolidated worksheet before it's submitted.
runbook.verify: Pick three numbers on the consolidated worksheet, open the source file and cell it cites, and check the number by eye — if any of the three don't match their cited cell, the whole worksheet needs a re-check before anyone signs it.

useCases.title: Make the podcast download CSV mean something
useCases.scenario: You get a monthly download CSV per show. Leadership asks 'how are the podcasts doing,' and you have never had a clean way to answer with a trend rather than a number.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Point Claude Code at the folder of monthly CSVs. Ask it to merge them, chart downloads per episode and per show over time, flag the episodes that outperformed their show's median, and write it into one workbook you re-run each month. Cheap, structural work — this is a Haiku or Sonnet job, not an Opus one.
useCases.timeSaved: A recurring 2-hour monthly chore becomes a 5-minute re-run
useCases.guardrail: Download platforms count differently. Ask Claude to state the definition it used for a 'download' and keep the platform's own methodology note attached to the report.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Collect the monthly download CSV for each show into one folder on your computer.
Open Claude Code and point it at that folder.
Ask it to merge the CSVs and chart downloads per episode and per show over time.
Ask it to flag which episodes outperformed their own show's median.
Ask it to state, in plain words, exactly how it's defining a "download" — and keep that definition attached to the report.
Write the result into one workbook you can point Claude Code at again next month, instead of starting over.
runbook.verify: Pick one show's total from the workbook and add up that show's downloads yourself straight from its monthly CSVs — if your total doesn't match, check whether the merge dropped a month or double-counted one.

useCases.title: Reconcile the traffic report against what actually aired
useCases.scenario: A sponsor claims their spots didn't run. You have the traffic log and the as-aired report, and comparing them by eye is how people lose afternoons.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Give Claude Code both exports and ask it to match scheduled spots against as-aired, list every discrepancy with date, time and spot ID, and produce a make-good candidate list. Tell it to show the matching rows for each discrepancy so you can spot-check the logic rather than trust it.
useCases.timeSaved: A 3-hour line-by-line reconciliation becomes ~25 minutes
useCases.guardrail: Claude finds mismatches; it does not draft the client response or write spot copy. Any copy that reaches air goes through FCC compliance review — no calls to action, no price or qualitative claims.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Export both the traffic log (what was scheduled) and the as-aired report (what actually ran).
Put both files in one folder on your computer and open Claude Code there.
Ask it to match scheduled spots against as-aired ones and list every discrepancy with date, time, and spot ID.
Ask it to show you the actual matching rows behind each discrepancy, not just its conclusion.
Ask it to produce a make-good candidate list from those discrepancies.
Spot-check a handful of the discrepancies yourself against the two original files before you tell the client anything.
Hand the confirmed list to whoever owns the client relationship — Claude drafts no response and no spot copy.
runbook.verify: Pick three discrepancies from the list and look up those exact date, time, and spot-ID rows in both original files yourself — if the mismatch isn't really there, the matching logic is wrong and the whole list needs a re-run.

tryThis.title: Your first real file — a pledge-drive readout, no formulas
tryThis.setup: Install the Claude desktop app and sign in with a paid Claude plan. Make a folder on your machine (call it 'station-work'), and put ONE anonymized spreadsheet in it — a pledge-drive export with donor names, emails, addresses and phone numbers deleted before it ever leaves your CRM. Numbers only. Open Claude Code and point it at that folder.
tryThis.prompt:
You are a development analyst at a small public radio station. In this folder there is one spreadsheet: our most recent pledge drive results. It has already been stripped of donor names and contact info — it is numbers only, and it must stay that way.

Read the file first and tell me what columns you actually see before you do anything else.

Then build me an Excel workbook called drive-readout.xlsx with:
1. A summary tab: total raised, number of gifts, average gift, median gift, new vs. renewing split.
2. An hour-by-hour tab showing when money actually came in.
3. A premium tab: which thank-you gifts drove the most dollars and which underperformed.
4. A short written 'what this tells us' section a board member could read in two minutes.

DO NOT invent, estimate, or fill in any number that is not in the source file. If a column I asked about doesn't exist, say so and leave it out — do not approximate. DO NOT add any donor names or contact information, even if you find some.

Before you show me the workbook, verify your work: recompute every total and average directly from the source rows, compare it to what you wrote into the workbook, and show me that comparison. If anything doesn't reconcile, fix it and re-check before you tell me you're done.

pitfalls: It gives you 60% on the first pass unless you ask it to verify. Bake the proof step into the prompt or you become the QA department.
Never point it at a raw CRM or donor export. Strip names, emails, addresses, phones and giving history first — anonymize before the file leaves your system, not after.
'It made a beautiful spreadsheet' is not 'the numbers are right.' Formatting quality and arithmetic quality are separate things. Spot-check totals against the source yourself the first few times.
Don't reflexively run everything on Opus. Sorting a CSV is a Haiku job; output tokens cost about 5x input, so re-generating a giant workbook repeatedly is where usage disappears.
Model names and prices in the video are a snapshot in time. Check Anthropic's current pricing before you build a budget on them.

keyQuotes.quote: How long would this have taken you to manually go pull out 75 videos, put all the analytics in here, color code it, design it? This literally took my AI agent about 10 minutes.
Make the AI prove its work.
If a human gave you this work, what would you do to approve it? Whatever you would do to verify it, chances are you can tell Claude Code to do that.

modules.title: Set Up Your Station's Workspace — And Its Guardrails
modules.kicker: CLAUDE.md, settings, keys, and what never leaves the building
modules.plainSummary: This module is where you stop chatting with an AI and start running it inside a workspace you control. You create a project folder, write a CLAUDE.md — a plain-English file the AI reads before it reads anything you type — and a .claude folder that holds your settings. You learn where secrets go (a .env file that never gets shared), what an API key actually is (a password), and how to hand out keys that can only do one narrow job. Then you learn the permission modes that decide how much Claude Code can do without asking you first, and the difference between bypass mode and sleeping well at night. Finally: what actually happens to your data when you hit enter.
modules.whyItMatters: Everything else in this course is a technique. This is the part that keeps you out of trouble. A station holds donor giving histories, unpublished investigations, personnel files, and CPB reporting data — and the difference between a tool that saves your development director ten hours and a tool that ends her career is entirely the setup you do in this module.

concepts.plain: A plain-text file in your project that Claude Code reads before it reads your message. It holds your rules, your context, and where things live.
concepts.stationTranslation: The station style guide the AI actually reads — every time, without being reminded. Your pronunciation list, your no-go phrases, your beat structure, your donor-data rules.

concepts.plain: A global file applies to every project on your machine. A project file applies only inside that one folder. You get both, and both get read.
concepts.stationTranslation: Global is house style — how the station writes, always. Project is show-level — the rules for pledge drive, or for this one investigation.

concepts.plain: A config folder next to your CLAUDE.md. Inside, settings.json lists what Claude Code is allowed to do and what it is flatly denied from doing.
concepts.stationTranslation: The door codes. CLAUDE.md is the staff handbook; settings.json is which rooms the badge actually opens. You describe the rules in English and Claude writes the file.

concepts.plain: An API key is a password that lets one tool pull data from another. It goes in a .env file, which stays on your machine and never gets shared or published.
concepts.stationTranslation: The key is a station credit card with your name on it. The .env file is the locked drawer. Never the script, never the email, never the shared folder.

concepts.plain: How much Claude Code can do before asking. The video shows auto (the default), manual permissions, accept edits, plan mode, and bypass permissions.
concepts.stationTranslation: Auto asks before anything consequential. Plan mode makes it show you the rundown first. Bypass is handing an intern the master key and going to lunch.

concepts.plain: Most tools let you create a key that can only do a few specific things, and only spend a set amount. Give the AI a key that can read but not delete.
concepts.stationTranslation: Your CRM key should let the AI read donor counts, never edit or delete a record. If it physically cannot delete, it cannot delete by accident.

concepts.plain: Claude is a closed model. Every message you send goes to Anthropic's servers to be processed. The data is there even when training is turned off.
concepts.stationTranslation: Treat the prompt box like a phone call to a vendor, not a note to yourself. Whatever you paste, you have handed to someone outside the station.

glossary.definition: Plain-English instructions file Claude Code reads before every message. Your standing rules and context.
Config file in the .claude folder listing what Claude Code is allowed and denied from doing.
File holding secrets like API keys. Kept local and excluded from anything you publish or share.
A password letting one tool pull data from another. Anyone holding it can use your account.
An API key restricted to specific capabilities and spending limits. Read-only, or one narrow function.
Setting controlling how often Claude Code stops to ask before acting. Auto is the default.
Mode where Claude Code never asks for approval. Also called 'dangerously skip permissions.' It means it.
Model Context Protocol. A standard way to plug Claude Code into another tool, like Gmail or QuickBooks.

useCases.title: A CRM key that can look but not touch
useCases.scenario: Your development director wants Claude Code to summarize lapsed-donor trends by segment, but the last thing anyone wants is an agent writing to the donor database.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Generate a read-only, scoped API key in your CRM — read access only, no edit, no delete, no export-all — and store it in .env. Then add a deny rule in settings.json so Claude Code can never run a delete or write command against it. Ask for aggregate counts, never named records.
useCases.timeSaved: A quarterly segment pull that took a week of back-and-forth with IT becomes an afternoon
useCases.guardrail: Never paste a donor export, name, address, email, or giving history into the prompt box. Aggregates only. If the key can write, it is the wrong key.
useCases.moduleTitle: Set Up Your Station's Workspace — And Its Guardrails

steps: In your CRM, create a new API key that is read-only — no edit, no delete, no export-all — and confirm in the CRM's own settings screen that it physically cannot write.
Save that key in a file called .env in your project folder, and never paste it anywhere else — not in CLAUDE.md, not in an email, not in Slack.
Open Claude Code in that folder and ask it to add a deny rule in settings.json blocking any write or delete command against the CRM.
Ask it to explain in plain English what that deny rule actually blocks, so you know it did what you asked before you rely on it.
Ask Claude for the donor segment summary you need — aggregate counts and totals only, never a request for named records.
Read the result back and confirm every line is a count or a total, with no donor name, email, or address anywhere in it.
runbook.verify: Go back to your CRM's key management screen and confirm the key shows read-only. If it shows any write or delete permission, delete the key, generate a new one, and redo the setup before using it again.

useCases.title: The newsroom style guide the AI actually follows
useCases.scenario: Every reporter re-explains the same rules to the AI: how you refer to the mayor's office, which sources need a second confirmation, that you never use 'slammed' in a lede.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Put all of it in a project CLAUDE.md once. When an output comes back wrong, tell Claude to update its own CLAUDE.md so it never repeats the mistake — the file gets smarter every time you correct it, instead of you retyping the same note.
useCases.timeSaved: Ten minutes of setup instructions per session drops to zero
useCases.guardrail: Unpublished investigative material and confidential source identities go nowhere near the prompt box. CLAUDE.md should say so explicitly, in writing, at the top.
useCases.moduleTitle: Set Up Your Station's Workspace — And Its Guardrails

steps: Open Claude Code in your newsroom project folder and ask it to create (or open) CLAUDE.md, with this as the very first rule: never accept unpublished investigative material or confidential source identities, in any form.
Tell Claude your house style — how you refer to the mayor's office, which sources need a second confirmation, banned words like 'slammed' — and have it write those rules into CLAUDE.md underneath.
Ask Claude to read the file back to you and confirm the no-go rule is still the first thing anyone sees when it opens the file.
Use Claude Code normally on a story that has no unpublished material or source names anywhere near it.
The next time an output comes back wrong — wrong style, wrong assumption — tell Claude to update CLAUDE.md itself so it doesn't repeat that mistake.
Ask it to show you exactly what changed in the file before you move on to the next story.
runbook.verify: Open CLAUDE.md and check that the unpublished-material rule is still the first rule in the file. If a later edit pushed it down or dropped it, put it back at the top before anyone uses this folder again.

useCases.title: Compliance rules baked into the workspace
useCases.scenario: An account executive drafts spot copy and keeps having to strip out calls to action and price claims that would violate FCC underwriting rules.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Write the FCC constraints directly into a project CLAUDE.md — no calls to action, no qualitative or comparative claims, no prices, no inducements to buy. Claude Code reads it before every draft, so the copy comes back closer to compliant on the first pass.
useCases.timeSaved: Three compliance rewrites per spot becomes roughly one
useCases.guardrail: A CLAUDE.md rule is a first filter, not a lawyer. Every spot still gets a human compliance review before it airs. Never treat the AI's 'this is compliant' as clearance.
useCases.moduleTitle: Set Up Your Station's Workspace — And Its Guardrails

steps: Open Claude Code in your underwriting project folder and open (or create) CLAUDE.md.
Write in the FCC constraints in plain language: no calls to action, no price or comparative claims, no inducements to buy — sponsor name and a factual description only.
Add a line requiring every draft to end with a plain statement that it hasn't been cleared and needs human compliance review.
Ask Claude to draft copy for a real or practice sponsor, then check the result against the rules you just wrote, line by line.
If anything slips past the rules, tell Claude exactly what it got wrong and have it add that specific example to CLAUDE.md.
Send every draft to your compliance reviewer before it airs, regardless of how clean Claude's version looks.
runbook.verify: Read the draft's closing note out loud. If it doesn't literally say the spot hasn't been cleared and needs a human review, the CLAUDE.md rule didn't take — fix it before the draft goes anywhere near a client.

useCases.title: Deciding what the station will and won't send to a vendor
useCases.scenario: Staff are already pasting things into AI tools and nobody has written down what is allowed. The board asks what your policy is.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Use this module's plain framing to write one: closed models send every prompt to a vendor's servers; turning off training does not delete the data; scoped keys and settings.json deny-rules are how you enforce limits technically instead of by memo. Put the resulting policy in your global CLAUDE.md so it applies to every project on every machine.
useCases.timeSaved: Turns a vague six-month policy debate into a one-page rule everyone can follow this week
useCases.guardrail: Donor PII, personnel files, unpublished journalism, legal correspondence, and anything under an NDA. Name them in the policy — 'be careful' is not a policy.
useCases.moduleTitle: Set Up Your Station's Workspace — And Its Guardrails

steps: List out, by name, everything that never goes in a prompt box: donor giving histories, personnel files, unpublished journalism, legal correspondence, anything under an NDA.

Open Claude Code and ask it to draft a one-page policy from that list, explaining in plain English that a closed AI model sends every prompt to a vendor's servers, and that turning off 'training' does not delete what was already sent.
Have Claude add the technical backstop as an example: a settings.json deny-rule and a scoped read-only key, so the policy isn't just a memo people forget.
Read the draft policy and correct anything it got wrong about your station's specific tools or data.
Put the final policy in your global CLAUDE.md so it applies on every project, on every machine, for everyone at the station.
Circulate the policy to staff and confirm each department head has actually read it.
runbook.verify: Ask two staffers in different departments to tell you, in their own words, what they're never allowed to paste into an AI tool. If they can't name the list from memory, the policy is filed away instead of understood — shorten it or post it somewhere visible.

useCases.title: Plan mode before it touches your CPB reporting files
useCases.scenario: Your Local Content and Service Report data lives in a folder of spreadsheets nobody wants an agent silently rewriting the week before the deadline.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Work in plan mode so Claude Code shows you exactly what it intends to change before it changes anything, and set deny rules in settings.json so it can never delete files in that directory. Then review the plan the way you'd review a rundown.
useCases.timeSaved: Removes the 'did it just overwrite my source data' panic entirely
useCases.guardrail: Never run bypass permissions mode in a folder containing the only copy of anything. Keep a backup outside the project directory.
useCases.moduleTitle: Set Up Your Station's Workspace — And Its Guardrails

steps: Before you do anything else, copy your Local Content and Service Report folder to a separate backup location outside the project directory.
Open Claude Code in the project folder and confirm it's running in Plan mode, not Bypass Permissions mode.
Ask Claude to add deny rules to settings.json so it can never delete files in that folder.
Describe the change you want made to the spreadsheets, and ask Claude to show you its plan before it touches anything.
Read the plan the way you'd read a rundown, line by line, and only approve it once every step makes sense.
After it runs, open the files and confirm the changes match the plan you approved.
runbook.verify: Compare today's file count and total row count in the folder against your backup. If either doesn't match what the approved plan said would change, stop and restore from backup before the deadline gets any closer.

tryThis.title: Build your station's CLAUDE.md in ten minutes
tryThis.setup: Make a new folder on your desktop — call it 'station-workspace' — and open Claude Code in it. When it asks whether you trust the folder, say yes (you made it). You do not need to know what a JSON file is; you are going to describe what you want in English and let Claude write the file.
tryThis.prompt:
I work at a small public radio station. I want to set up this project properly before I do any real work in it.

Please do three things:

1. Create a CLAUDE.md in this folder. Use the example structure I'm about to describe: a short section on who we are, a house style section, a hard 'never do this' section covering donor data and unpublished journalism, and a section on where files live. Ask me questions if you need details about the station — don't guess.

2. Create a .claude folder with a settings.json. I want you to be explicitly DENIED from deleting any file in this project, and denied from running any command that removes files. I don't know the syntax — write it for me and then explain in plain English what each rule does.

3. Create a .env file with a placeholder for an API key, and make sure it can never be committed or shared. Explain to me in two sentences why the key goes there and not in CLAUDE.md.

When you're done, show me all three files and walk me through them like I've never seen a config file before — because I haven't.

modules.exampleClaudeMd:
# CLAUDE.md — WXYZ Public Radio

## Who we are
WXYZ is a community-licensed public radio station. Small team, no IT department.
Staff using this workspace: news, membership, underwriting, music, and the GM.
When in doubt about what a role needs, ask — don't assume.

## Never — no exceptions
These are not preferences. Do not do these even if I ask in the moment.
- Never accept, process, or repeat donor PII: names, addresses, emails, phone numbers,
  giving history, credit card data, or CRM exports. If I paste any of it, STOP and tell me
  I just made a mistake. Aggregate, de-identified numbers only.
- Never accept unpublished investigative material, confidential source identities,
  or anything under embargo.
- Never accept personnel files, salary data, or legal correspondence.
- Never publish, post, send, or schedule anything. You draft. A human sends.
- Never delete files in this project. Never run a command that removes files.

## Editorial rules (news)
- You do not write publishable journalism. You research, transcribe, summarize, prep
  questions, and draft. A human reporter edits and verifies every fact before air or post.
- Every factual claim you give me needs a source I can check. No source, mark it UNVERIFIED.
- Never invent a quote, a name, a date, or a statistic. If you don't know, say you don't know.
- House style: AP, with our exceptions in /style/ap-exceptions.md.
- Banned in ledes: "slammed," "blasted," "amid growing concerns," "in a stunning move."

## Underwriting rules (FCC)
Underwriting is NOT advertising. Any spot copy you draft must have:
- No call to action ("visit," "call now," "stop by," "buy")
- No price or value information, no discounts, no "free"
- No qualitative or comparative claims ("best," "finest," "leading")
- No inducement to buy
You are a first filter, not clearance. Every spot goes to a human for compliance review.
Flag anything you're unsure about rather than smoothing it over.

## Music & licensing
- Do not state ASCAP/BMI/SESAC/SoundExchange rules from memory. If I ask about reporting
  requirements, look them up and cite the source, or tell me you don't know.
- Local artist spellings and pronunciations: /music/pronunciation.md. Check it before drafting
  any host copy. Getting a local artist's name wrong is worse than not mentioning them.

## Voice
Warm, plain, specific. We sound like a person talking to a neighbor, not a press release.
- No hype. No "delve," "tapestry," "in today's fast-paced world," "it's not just X, it's Y."
- Short sentences. Say the thing.
- Never use an em dash where a period works.

## Where things live
- /news/          drafts, interview prep, transcripts (NO unpublished investigations)
- /membership/    pledge scripts, campaign copy (AGGREGATE NUMBERS ONLY)
- /underwriting/  spot copy drafts, FCC reference
- /music/         playlists, host copy, pronunciation guide
- /grants/        CPB LCSR drafts, board reporting
- /style/         AP exceptions, banned phrases
- .env            API keys. Never read these values back to me in chat.

## How to get better
When I tell you an output was wrong, don't just fix that one output. Update this file so
you don't make the same mistake next time, and tell me what you changed.
pitfalls: Bypass permissions mode is exactly what the name says — Claude Code never stops to ask. The video's own team had an agent email a discount code to 150,000 people, and is honest about the real cause: not the permission mode, but an agent with access to too many tools. Scoped keys are your first line of defense; permission modes are the second. Don't run bypass on a station machine that touches real files.

Turning off 'help improve Claude' in your privacy settings stops training on your data. It does not mean the vendor never had it. Every prompt still traveled to their servers. Decide what your station is willing to send before you send it, not after.

Your API key is a password. Not 'kind of like' a password — it is one. Anyone holding it can spend your budget and read your account. It belongs in .env, never in CLAUDE.md, never in a shared drive, never in Slack.

A CLAUDE.md rule is guidance, not a lock. If something must never happen — deleting files, writing to your CRM — enforce it in settings.json deny rules and in the key's own permissions, where the AI physically cannot get around it.

Don't build a perfect CLAUDE.md on day one. The video is clear that it grows: you get a bad output, you tell Claude to update its own instructions so it never happens again. That loop is the whole point.

keyQuotes.quote: This allows Claude to just do everything. It will never stop and say, 'Hey, is this okay? Can I do this?' It will just do everything.
Would you give a new hire a credit card and say, hey, don't spend anything — but with this card you could? That just makes no sense.
For the company that you work for or your own business, you should not be sending over private sensitive data.

modules.title: The AI Operating System — Wiring Claude Into the Tools Your Station Already Runs On
modules.kicker: Google Workspace, CLIs vs MCPs, and Skills

sourceChapters.title: Connecting Google Workspace: CLIs vs MCPs
Installing in the Terminal + Free Resources

modules.plainSummary: Up to now Claude Code has been working with files you hand it. This module connects it to the systems your station actually lives in — Gmail, Drive, Docs, Sheets, Calendar — so it can go get the information itself instead of waiting for you to paste it. You'll see the three ways a tool gets connected (a one-click connector, an MCP, or a command-line tool), and why the course argues for the plumbing you own rather than the plumbing a single app owns. Then it gets to the biggest idea in the whole course: Skills. A Skill is a plain-text folder that holds one of your station's procedures — how underwriting copy gets written, how a host preps a show — and Claude picks it up on its own the moment the work matches. Do the job once with Claude, then tell it to save what you just did as a Skill, and the procedure stops living in one person's head.
modules.whyItMatters: Every station has a dozen procedures that exist only because one person remembers them — the LCSR spreadsheet quirks, the way underwriting copy gets phrased so it clears compliance, the show-prep ritual a host does at 5:30am. A Skill turns that into a written, improvable file that anyone on staff can run and that survives the person leaving. And because Claude can reach into the Google Workspace your station already pays for, those procedures run against real logs, real calendars, and real drafts instead of re-typed copies.

concepts.plain: Instead of hopping between fifteen browser tabs all day, you talk to one place, and it goes and uses the tools for you. Your project folder grows into that hub over time.
concepts.stationTranslation: One window instead of Gmail, Drive, the music log, the CRM, and the traffic system all open at once. You ask; it goes and fetches.

concepts.plain: A tool you run by typing a command instead of clicking buttons. Same actions a person would do in the web app, just triggered by text — which is exactly how Claude works anyway.
concepts.stationTranslation: Like the automation system's command syntax versus its click-around screen. Claude drives Google Workspace by typing commands, not by pretending to be a browser.

concepts.plain: One free open-source Google tool that gives Claude reach into Gmail, Drive, Docs, Sheets, Slides, and Calendar. One install and one login instead of six separate hookups.
concepts.stationTranslation: The one connection that unlocks most of your day: draft in Docs, read the log in Sheets, triage the info@ inbox, find a free hour on the GM's calendar.

concepts.plain: Three ways to plug a tool in. The video's argument for the CLI: one interface across many apps, clean structured output, it updates itself, and it eats far less of Claude's attention than a pile of separate MCP tool definitions.
concepts.stationTranslation: A connector is the easiest today and the most painful the day you switch tools. Keys and CLIs you set up yourself move with you.

concepts.plain: A folder holding a SKILL.md file — a written recipe for one procedure. Claude reads the recipe when the job matches, follows the steps, and you edit the recipe when the output isn't right.
concepts.stationTranslation: The station SOP, finally written down somewhere that actually does the work. 'How we write underwriting copy' becomes a file, not a person's memory.

concepts.plain: Each Skill starts with a few lines saying what it is and when to use it. Claude skims only those lines across all your Skills, then fully reads the one it needs.
concepts.stationTranslation: Like a shelf of binders with clear spine labels. Claude reads the spines, pulls one binder, ignores the other thirty-nine.

concepts.term: Building a Skill after the fact
concepts.plain: Two ways to make one. Plan it up front — 'step one, step two, step three' — or just do the task with Claude, then say: that's something I do every week, turn it into a Skill.
concepts.stationTranslation: The second way is better for stations. Nobody can describe how they prep a show. But they can do it once and let Claude write it down.

glossary.definition: Command-line interface. A tool you operate by typing commands instead of clicking. Free, fast, and easy for Claude to drive.
A standard plug that lets an AI talk to an app. Useful, but each one loads its own set of tools into Claude's attention.
The one-click sign-in list inside the Claude app. Easiest to set up, but the connection lives in that app, not in your files.
Set up so your work survives switching AI apps. Files, folders, and your own keys move; app-specific connectors don't.
A folder with a SKILL.md file describing one procedure. Claude loads it on its own when the request matches.
The few labeled lines at the top of a SKILL.md — name, description, when to use it. Claude reads these to decide.
Claude skims every Skill's description, then fully reads only the one it needs. Keeps forty Skills from crowding out your actual work.
The 'Sign in with Google, allow access' step. How you grant Claude reach into your own Workspace account, revocably.

useCases.title: An "underwriting-copy" Skill that knows the FCC line
useCases.scenario: Sponsor copy gets written by whoever's free, then bounces back from compliance review because someone slipped in a qualitative claim or a call to action.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Build a SKILL.md that holds your station's actual rules: value-neutral language, no CTA, no price or comparative claims, required sponsor identification, your 15-second and 30-second templates, plus three approved examples. From then on, 'draft copy for the new sponsor' pulls that Skill automatically and produces two or three variants against the rules. Every time compliance catches something, you add one line to the Skill and it never happens again.
useCases.timeSaved: Copy-and-rewrite cycles drop from three rounds to about one
useCases.guardrail: A human still signs off on every spot before it airs. The Skill is a first draft and a rule reminder, not FCC clearance — Claude does not know your license.
useCases.moduleTitle: The AI Operating System — Wiring Claude Into the Tools Your Station Already Runs On

steps: In Claude Code, ask it to create a Skill folder — .claude/skills/underwriting-copy — with a SKILL.md that describes exactly when to use it: drafting sponsor spot copy.
Give it your actual rules: no call to action, no price or comparative claims, required sponsor identification, and your 15- and 30-second templates.
Add two or three approved past examples to the Skill folder so Claude has real copy to match the voice against.
Require every draft the Skill produces to close with a plain line stating it hasn't been cleared and needs human compliance review.
Test it: ask for copy for a new sponsor without naming the Skill, and confirm Claude pulled it in on its own.
Every time compliance catches something the Skill missed, add that exact example to the SKILL.md so it never slips through again.
runbook.verify: Ask for sponsor copy without mentioning the Skill by name. If Claude doesn't apply the rules and templates on its own, the Skill's description isn't specific enough for Claude to find it — rewrite that description line.

useCases.title: A "show-prep" Skill that builds the host's doc before dawn
useCases.scenario: A host spends the first hour of every morning stitching together the music log, local headlines, and what's happening in town into a prep doc.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: With the Google Workspace CLI connected, one Skill reads today's log out of the Sheet, pulls the station's local-news doc from Drive, and writes a formatted prep Doc — not raw markdown dumped into a page, but an actual Google Doc with headers and links. The host runs it with one sentence, then spends the saved hour on the parts only a human can do: the story behind the track, the phrasing, the local voice.
useCases.timeSaved: A 60-minute morning stitch-together becomes about 10 minutes of review
useCases.guardrail: Anything the host says on air is the host's. Claude assembles the folder; it does not write the break. Check every artist name and date — it will get one wrong eventually.
useCases.moduleTitle: The AI Operating System — Wiring Claude Into the Tools Your Station Already Runs On

steps: Connect the Google Workspace CLI to your Claude Code project so it can reach your Sheets and Drive.
Ask Claude to create a Skill — .claude/skills/show-prep — that reads today's row out of the music log Sheet and pulls the station's local-news doc from Drive.
Tell it the exact shape of the finished prep doc you want: headers, sections, links — not a raw markdown dump.
Have it write the result as an actual Google Doc, saved where the host already looks for it, not just a chat reply.
Run the Skill with one sentence and read the whole doc yourself before the host does.
Check every artist name, song title, and date against the source log — the Skill will get one wrong eventually.
runbook.verify: Cross-check three random facts in the prep doc — an artist name, a date, a headline — against the actual Sheet and Drive doc. If any don't match, fix the Skill before the host reads it live.

useCases.title: An "lcsr-helper" Skill for the CPB report
useCases.scenario: The Local Content & Service Report comes due, and last year's answers live in an email thread with a person who has since left the station.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Turn the report into a Skill: the actual CPB questions, the tone that worked last year, where each piece of evidence lives in Drive, and the station's standing description of its service area. Claude pulls last year's submission, pulls this year's supporting docs, and drafts each narrative section with the source noted beside it. The Skill gets edited as the questions change, so next year's report starts from the real procedure instead of from scratch.
useCases.timeSaved: A multi-week scramble becomes a few focused days of verification
useCases.guardrail: Every number in a CPB filing must be traced back to your own records by a human. Claude drafts narrative; it does not certify. Never let it invent a figure to fill a blank.
useCases.moduleTitle: The AI Operating System — Wiring Claude Into the Tools Your Station Already Runs On

steps: Gather what the Skill will need: the actual CPB report questions, last year's submission, this year's supporting documents in Drive, and your station's standing service-area description.
Ask Claude to build a Skill — .claude/skills/lcsr-helper — that pulls those documents and drafts each narrative section with the source noted beside it.
Tell the Skill explicitly, in writing: never invent a number to fill a blank — leave it blank and flag it if the source isn't there.
Run the Skill and get a full draft of this year's report with every section sourced.
Trace every single number in the draft back to your own records, one at a time, before accepting it.
Edit the SKILL.md itself wherever this year's questions changed from last year's, so next year starts from the corrected version.
runbook.verify: Pick five numbers at random from the draft and find each one in its cited source document. If even one doesn't match, or has no real source behind it, do not submit until you've traced every remaining figure by hand.

useCases.title: A "pledge-recap" Skill for the board packet
useCases.scenario: The drive ends Sunday night. The board wants a summary Tuesday, and the person who knows the spreadsheet is asleep.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: A Skill that reads the aggregate results Sheet — totals by day, by hour, by challenge grant, new vs renewing counts — and turns it into the board-ready narrative your GM actually presents: what worked, what fell flat, how it compares to last spring. The Skill stores your board's preferred format, so the recap comes out in the shape they already read.
useCases.timeSaved: A two-day scramble becomes an afternoon
useCases.guardrail: Aggregate numbers only. No donor names, no addresses, no giving histories, no CRM export ever goes into the folder Claude can read. Strip to totals before Claude sees the file, and say so inside the Skill itself.
useCases.moduleTitle: The AI Operating System — Wiring Claude Into the Tools Your Station Already Runs On

steps: Before building anything, strip the results spreadsheet down to aggregates only — totals by day, by hour, by challenge grant, new vs. renewing counts — with no donor names, addresses, or giving histories in the file.
Put that stripped file in the folder Claude Code can see, and confirm the original donor-level export stays outside that folder entirely.
Ask Claude to build a Skill — .claude/skills/pledge-recap — that reads the aggregate Sheet and writes the board-ready narrative in your GM's preferred format.
Write the same rule into the SKILL.md itself: this Skill only ever reads aggregate totals, never a donor-level export.
Run the Skill and get the recap — what worked, what fell flat, how it compares to last spring.
Read the recap and confirm every figure is a total or a count, checked against the aggregate Sheet, before it goes to the board.
runbook.verify: Open the aggregate file the Skill actually read and search it for an @ sign or anything that looks like a person's name. If either shows up, the file wasn't stripped — redo it before the Skill touches it again.

useCases.title: Inbox triage on the station's shared account
useCases.scenario: The info@ and news tips inboxes have 200 unread messages and nobody owns them, so real listener questions and real story tips die in there.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: This is the exact demo in the course, retargeted: with Workspace connected, a Skill scores unread mail against what it knows about the station's priorities and sorts it into high / needs action / ignore, with a short brief. Start it in read-only mode — brief only, no labels, no deletion — and only let it take action once you've watched it be right twenty times.
useCases.timeSaved: A 200-message backlog triaged in ~15 minutes instead of a lost afternoon
useCases.guardrail: Read-only until proven. Never let it auto-archive or auto-reply on a public-facing account — one deleted news tip or one auto-reply to a major donor is a bad day.
useCases.moduleTitle: The AI Operating System — Wiring Claude Into the Tools Your Station Already Runs On

steps: Connect Gmail through Claude Code using an account that only has access to the info@ and news-tips inboxes you intend to triage — nothing broader.
Ask Claude to build a Skill that reads unread mail and sorts it into high / needs action / ignore, with a short brief for each — read-only, no labels applied, nothing deleted.
Run it against today's backlog and read the brief for every message it calls 'ignore,' not just the ones it flags as high.
Compare its calls against what you already know about real listener questions or story tips sitting in there.
Keep it in read-only mode and repeat this check across at least twenty runs before you trust its sorting.
Only after twenty clean runs, consider letting it apply labels — and never let it auto-archive or auto-reply on a public-facing account, even then.
runbook.verify: Manually open every message the Skill marked 'ignore' at least once. If a real tip or donor question is sitting in that bucket, it isn't ready to act on its own yet — stay read-only.

tryThis.title: Turn one thing you already did into a Skill
tryThis.setup: Pick a task you actually finished with Claude Code this week — a rewritten sponsor spot, a cleaned-up spreadsheet, a summarized meeting. Stay in that same conversation, so Claude still has the steps in front of it.
tryThis.prompt:
Look back at everything we just did in this conversation. That's a process I repeat at the station — roughly every week. Turn it into a Skill I can reuse.

Before you write it, ask me 3-5 questions about the parts you had to guess at, so the Skill captures how we actually do it here and not just how it went once.

Then create the Skill folder with a SKILL.md that has:
- front matter with a clear name and a description that says exactly when to use it
- the numbered steps we followed
- our station's rules and preferences (format, tone, what we never do)
- a short section on what a human must check before anything goes out

When it's written, show it to me in plain English so I can tell you what you got wrong.

pitfalls: Connectors are the trap. The one-click sign-in list is easiest today and the most painful the day you switch apps — the video is blunt that you'd have to rebuild every connection from scratch. Set up your own keys and CLIs and your plumbing moves with you.

The Google Workspace CLI is a Google open-source beta, not an officially supported Google product. The course says so out loud, and the repo warns of breaking changes. Fine for drafting and reading; do not build a compliance deadline on top of it without a fallback.

Setup will not work on the first try. The video's own walkthrough hits a dead end and has to back up and do it manually. Expect a Google Cloud project, an OAuth screen, and a round of enabling APIs one at a time. Budget an hour and let Claude read the docs for you.

A Skill is never finished. The video's whole point is the loop: run it, judge it, edit the file. A Skill written once and never corrected will confidently repeat your worst assumptions — and it may behave differently when the model updates, so re-run your important ones after each new release.

Connecting Gmail and Drive means Claude can now see whatever is in Gmail and Drive. Before you authorize it, know what lives in that Drive. If donor exports, personnel files, or unredacted tips are sitting in a shared folder, move them out first — or use an account that never had access.

keyQuotes.quote: It's basically just an SOP. However you decide you want to do something, a skill just packages it up so that you can consistently do that again every time.
Becoming tool agnostic is the most important thing you can do, because these tools evolve so quick. All we're doing here is building a bunch of folders and files.
The hardest part, honestly, is just identifying what tools you need. Go look at the tabs you have open right now. What tools are you visiting frequently? That's what you want to connect.

skillAnatomy.whatItIs: A Skill is a written procedure Claude can pick up and follow — a station SOP saved as a plain text file. It is not a prompt you paste each time and it is not a setting you toggle. A prompt is a request you make once; a Skill is a recipe that stays on the shelf, gets used whenever the job matches, and gets edited when the output isn't right. The course's analogy is a recipe card: the first time you make it you follow every step, then you decide you want more chocolate chips, so you change the card — and now everyone who reaches for that card gets your version.
skillAnatomy.whereItLives: A folder inside your project, under .claude/skills/. Each Skill is its own subfolder — .claude/skills/underwriting-copy/ — containing a file named SKILL.md. That markdown file is the master instruction. Alongside it you can drop anything the procedure needs: a reference file of your FCC rules, an examples file of approved copy, the station style guide, a spreadsheet template. Because it is all just folders and text files, a Skill can be emailed to another station, checked into a shared Drive, or carried over to a different AI tool — the course notes that other tools look in a differently-named folder, but the files themselves transfer.
skillAnatomy.whenClaudeLoadsIt: On its own, when the work matches. Every SKILL.md opens with a few labeled lines — the front matter — giving its name and a description of exactly when to use it. Claude skims only those descriptions across all your Skills, then fully reads the one that fits. That's called progressive disclosure, and it's why a station can accumulate forty Skills without them crowding out the actual work: forty spine labels get read, one binder gets opened. So 'draft copy for the new credit union sponsor' triggers the underwriting Skill without you naming it. You can also call one deliberately with a slash command, like /underwriting-copy.
skillAnatomy.exampleSkillMd:
---
name: underwriting-copy
description: Drafts FCC-compliant underwriting announcements for station sponsors. Use when someone asks for sponsor copy, a spot script, an underwriting announcement, a credit, or says "write copy for [sponsor]".
---

# Underwriting Copy

Draft announcements that identify a sponsor without promoting them. We are a
noncommercial station. Underwriting is not advertising, and copy that reads like
advertising puts the license at risk.

## Before you write

1. Ask for the sponsor name, what they do, their location, and any URL or phone
   number they want included. Do not guess at these.
2. Ask which lengths are needed. Default to one :15 and one :30.
3. Read `rules.md` in this folder. Read `approved-examples.md` for our voice.

## The rules (non-negotiable)

NEVER include:
- A call to action. No "visit", "call today", "stop by", "check out", "don't miss".
- Price, discounts, or any deal. No "$5 off", "free consultation", "affordable".
- Qualitative or comparative claims. No "best", "leading", "award-winning",
  "trusted", "finest", "#1". Value-neutral language only.
