- Superlatives about the sponsor's product or service, in any form.

ALWAYS include:
- The sponsor's legal name, clearly stated.
- A value-neutral description of what they actually do.
- The station's standard closing tag from `rules.md`.

Allowed and encouraged:
- Established factual descriptors ("serving Milwaukee since 1974").
- A location. A slogan the sponsor already uses in general circulation.
- A URL or phone number, stated plainly, with no urgency attached.

## Write

Produce two options per requested length so the sponsor has a choice.
Stay inside our word counts: :15 is 35-40 words, :30 is 70-80 words.
Read each draft back and check it against the NEVER list, line by line.

## Output format

For each option:
- The copy, exactly as it would be read on air
- The word count and estimated read time
- A short compliance note: which rules you checked, and anything you were unsure
  about — flag it rather than smoothing it over

## Human review — required

End every run with this line, plainly:

  "This is a draft. It has not been cleared. Underwriting must review and
   approve before this airs."

You do not clear copy. You draft it. If the sponsor pushes for language that
breaks a rule above, say so directly and hand it to a human — do not soften the
rule to make the sponsor happy.

## Keep this Skill current

When compliance rejects a line, ask me why, then add it to the NEVER list with
the real example. This file should get harder to break every month.

modules.title: What the AI Can Hold in Its Head — and What Only Lives in Yours
modules.kicker: Context, memory, and mapping the process
modules.plainSummary: Every AI session has a finite amount of room to hold information — the context window. Fill it up and the assistant gets noticeably worse: it forgets what you told it an hour ago, contradicts itself, and starts making things up. This module teaches you to see how full it is, to reset it deliberately with a written 'session handoff' instead of losing your work, and to make Claude remember the durable stuff (your preferences, your station's rules) in files rather than in the conversation. Then it turns to the least glamorous and most valuable skill in the whole course: writing down how you actually do a thing, step by step, before you ask a machine to help you do it. The video's line is blunt — you can't automate what you can't map.
modules.whyItMatters: The number one thing non-coders get wrong is treating the AI like it has infinite memory, then blaming the model when a four-hour session turns to mush. And the second thing they get wrong is trying to automate a process nobody has ever written down. At a station, the unwritten process usually lives in exactly one person's head — the traffic person who knows why the 7:20 spot always moves, the membership person who knows which donors get a call and not an email. Mapping that process so an AI can follow it is the same document you'd need if that person left tomorrow. The AI project is the excuse; the succession plan is the payoff.

concepts.plain: The total amount of text the AI can hold in mind at once — your messages, its replies, the files it opened, its own instructions. It is large but finite, and it fills up.
concepts.stationTranslation: The producer's prep folder for one show. Everything the host can reach for while on air. Stuff more in and eventually they can't find anything.

concepts.plain: As the window fills, answer quality drifts. The model gets confused, forgets earlier decisions, repeats itself. It isn't broken — it's overloaded.
concepts.stationTranslation: Hour eleven of pledge drive. The board op is still technically there, but the errors start. The video's analogy: a truck driver past their legal hours.

concepts.plain: /clear wipes the session to zero — a fresh start with no memory of the conversation. /compact summarizes and keeps going. Claude Code also auto-compacts when you get close to full.
concepts.stationTranslation: /clear is a new prep folder. /compact is cramming the old one into a one-page summary — helpful, but you lose detail you may still need.

concepts.plain: Before you clear, ask Claude to write a summary of where you started, decisions locked, what shipped, key files, what's still open. You copy that, run /clear, and paste it into the fresh session.
concepts.stationTranslation: The shift-change note. The morning board op leaves a page for the afternoon: here's what aired, here's what broke, here's what still needs a decision.

concepts.plain: Claude Code has auto-memory: it writes durable facts and your corrections into small markdown files it can look up later, indexed by a MEMORY.md file. CLAUDE.md holds the rules; memory holds learned facts.
concepts.stationTranslation: CLAUDE.md is the station's style guide and operations policy. Memory is the sticky notes: 'this GM hates the word leverage,' 'we always spell it Wisconsin Avenue.'

concepts.plain: The one bottleneck that actually limits your output. Fixing anything else just makes you busy. The video's test: if five times the volume showed up tomorrow, what breaks first?
concepts.stationTranslation: If your membership doubled overnight, what snaps — the thank-you letters? The traffic log? Automate that. Not the thing that demoed well on LinkedIn.

concepts.term: You can't automate what you can't map
concepts.plain: Before you build anything, write the process down: what triggers it, what you do, in what order, with what judgment calls. If you can't explain it to an intern, an AI can't do it either.
concepts.stationTranslation: This is the thing nobody has time for and everybody needs. The map you write so Claude can run the process is the same map you'd hand a new hire on day one.

glossary.definition: A chunk of text, roughly three-quarters of a word. Context windows and usage limits are measured in tokens, not pages.
The quality drop that happens as a session's context window fills up. Also called 'the dumb zone.'
Claude Code command that summarizes the conversation so far and keeps going. Runs automatically when you near the limit — often too late.
Wipes the session clean. Fresh context, zero memory of what you just did — so hand off first.
Claude Code writing durable facts and your corrections into small markdown files across sessions. Toggle it with /memory in the terminal.
The index file that points to those individual memory files. One fact per file; MEMORY.md is the table of contents.
The one number you pick before you build, that tells you afterward whether the build was worth it.
Work only compounds at the bottleneck. Unclog the biggest one, and the next one becomes the biggest.

useCases.title: Get the log out of one person's head
useCases.scenario: Your traffic person has run the log for eleven years. Nobody else fully understands the rotation rules, the make-good policy, or why certain sponsors never sit next to each other. They're talking about retiring.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Sit with them and use Claude Code as the scribe: it interviews them, asks the follow-up questions you'd forget, and writes the process to a plain markdown file — triggers, steps, judgment calls, exceptions. Two sessions in, you have a real operations document. Only then do you look at what part of it could be automated.
useCases.timeSaved: A process doc that has been 'on the list' for a decade gets drafted in two 90-minute sittings
useCases.guardrail: The retiring expert reviews and signs off on the map. Claude will happily fill a gap with a plausible-sounding rule that isn't your rule — every step needs a human 'yes, that's how we do it.'
useCases.moduleTitle: What the AI Can Hold in Its Head — and What Only Lives in Yours

steps: Sit down with your traffic person for 90 minutes, phone and distractions away, in a folder on your computer called station-work.
Open Claude Code in that folder and tell it: interview us about how the log actually gets built, one question at a time, and don't write anything until we say we're done.
Let it ask, and let the traffic person answer out loud — including the parts that feel too obvious to say, like why two sponsors never sit back to back.
When it guesses at a rule instead of asking, stop it and correct it on the spot — a plausible-sounding rule that isn't your rule is worse than no rule.
Have it write everything to a plain file called traffic-process.md: triggers, steps, judgment calls, exceptions.
Read the whole file out loud together and have the traffic person mark every line that isn't quite right.
Fix those lines, then have the traffic person sign and date the bottom of the file.
Only after it's signed off, ask what part of the written process — not the whole job — could actually be automated.
runbook.verify: The traffic person reads the finished file start to finish and says, out loud, 'yes, that's how we do it' for every step. If there's one step they hesitate on, that step isn't done yet.

useCases.title: Map the drive before you automate the drive
useCases.scenario: Pledge drive prep is a two-week scramble every time, and the sequence lives in a shared inbox and one person's memory. Leadership keeps asking whether AI could help.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Do the video's audit first: write down every trigger in a drive week (pitch block starts, gift comes in, thank-you goes out, premium ships) and which of them happens most. Pick the single biggest clog and a north star number — 'we cut thank-you turnaround from nine days to two.' Then build only for that.
useCases.timeSaved: Prevents three months of building the wrong tool
useCases.guardrail: Map the process using descriptions, not data. No donor names, addresses, giving history, or CRM exports go into Claude — the map says 'look up the donor's last gift,' it does not contain the donor's last gift.
useCases.moduleTitle: What the AI Can Hold in Its Head — and What Only Lives in Yours

steps: Before you open anything, agree with your team: this map describes what happens, in words — no donor names, addresses, giving history, or CRM exports ever go into Claude.
Open Claude Code in a folder on your machine called station-work.
List out loud every trigger that happens during a drive week: pitch block starts, a gift comes in, a thank-you goes out, a premium ships.
Ask Claude to help you mark which of those triggers happens most often, and where the backlog actually piles up.
Pick the single biggest clog and write down one plain-English number that would prove you fixed it, like 'thank-you turnaround drops from nine days to two.'
Have Claude write the clog, the number, and the trigger list to a file called drive-map.md — descriptions only, still no donor data.
Read the file back and confirm every line refers to a system or a step, never to a specific person's information.
Take that one file to leadership before anyone writes a line of code or buys a tool.
runbook.verify: Search drive-map.md for an @ sign, a dollar amount, or a full name. If any of those show up, donor data leaked into the map and it needs to be rewritten in descriptions only.

useCases.title: A three-day investigation that doesn't lose the thread
useCases.scenario: A reporter is working a records-heavy story across several days — hundreds of pages of council minutes, a spreadsheet of contracts, a working outline. By day two the assistant is contradicting its own earlier summaries.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: That's context rot, not a bad model. At the end of each working session, ask for a session handoff: what we established, what's still unverified, which documents we've read, what to check next. Copy it, /clear, paste it into a fresh session. The reporter picks up sharp instead of dragging two days of sludge along.
useCases.timeSaved: A day-three session that used to be useless becomes as sharp as day one
useCases.guardrail: The handoff note is a research memo, not a fact-check. Every claim in it still gets verified against the primary document by the reporter before it goes anywhere near air or print.
useCases.moduleTitle: What the AI Can Hold in Its Head — and What Only Lives in Yours

steps: Keep unverified claims and any source's identity out of anything you'll paste elsewhere later — the handoff note is a work summary, not a place to stash sensitive material.
At the end of each day's working session in Claude Code, before you close it, ask: write me a handoff — what we established, what's still unverified, which documents we've read, what to check next.
Read that handoff over and fix anything it got wrong or overstated — it will sound confident even about things that aren't confirmed.
Copy the handoff text somewhere safe, like a plain text file on your machine, not just left in the chat.
Run /clear to wipe the session — do this only after the handoff is safely copied out.
Paste the handoff into the fresh session as your first message the next day.
Before you write or air anything, check every claim in the handoff against the original document yourself.
runbook.verify: Pick three claims from the handoff at random and find each one in the original council minutes, contract, or document. If you can't find one, that claim doesn't get used until you can.

useCases.title: Write down how the LCSR actually gets done
useCases.scenario: The CPB Local Content and Service Report gets assembled every year by whoever is least able to say no. The steps are re-invented annually — which numbers come from where, who signs off, what counts as a partnership.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: During this year's assembly, keep a Claude Code session open and narrate as you go: 'this figure comes from the underwriting spreadsheet, this narrative gets reviewed by the GM.' Have it write the process to a file. Next year that file is the starting point, not a blank page — and it's the same file you'd hand to a successor.
useCases.timeSaved: Next year's assembly starts from a written process instead of institutional archaeology
useCases.guardrail: Claude does not know CPB's current requirements. The map documents your station's internal process; the actual reporting rules come from CPB's published guidance and a human confirms them each cycle.
useCases.moduleTitle: What the AI Can Hold in Its Head — and What Only Lives in Yours

steps: Open Claude Code in a folder called station-work and keep it open the whole time you assemble this year's LCSR.
As you pull each figure, narrate it out loud into the chat: 'this number comes from the underwriting spreadsheet,' 'this narrative gets reviewed by the GM.'
As you decide what counts as a partnership or a program, say the reasoning behind that call too, not just the answer.
At the end, ask Claude to turn everything you narrated into a written process file called lcsr-process.md: where each figure comes from, who signs off, what counts as what.
Read it against what you actually did this year and correct anything it smoothed over or guessed at.
Save that file somewhere the next person assembling the LCSR will actually find it, not just in this year's folder.
Note in the file that CPB's current requirements still need to be confirmed fresh from CPB's published guidance every year — this file documents your station's process, not CPB's rules.
runbook.verify: Hand lcsr-process.md to someone at the station who didn't help assemble this year's report and ask them to find where any one figure comes from. If they can't find it in the file, the file is missing a step.

useCases.title: Find the clog before you buy the tool
useCases.scenario: The GM is getting pitched AI tools from every direction and has budget for roughly one. Every vendor demo looks impressive.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Run the video's constraint question with your department heads: if we got five times the volume tomorrow — five times the members, five times the story pitches, five times the underwriting inquiries — what breaks first? Write down the answers. That list, plus a north star number for each, is your actual AI roadmap, and it's yours rather than a vendor's.
useCases.timeSaved: One afternoon of honest questions replaces a year of shiny-object procurement
useCases.guardrail: Don't automate a broken process — you'll just scale the breakage. If a step is broken, fix the step before you speed it up.
useCases.moduleTitle: What the AI Can Hold in Its Head — and What Only Lives in Yours

steps: Get your department heads in a room for one afternoon, no vendors, no demos.

Ask each one the same question: if we got five times the volume tomorrow — five times the members, five times the story pitches, five times the underwriting inquiries — what breaks first in your department?
Write down every answer, in their words, without smoothing it into vendor-speak.
Open Claude Code afterward and have it help you organize those answers into a short list, one clog per department.
For each clog, agree on one plain number that would prove you'd fixed it — not a feeling, an actual measurable thing.
Rank the list so the biggest, most damaging clog is at the top.
Treat that ranked list as your actual AI roadmap for the year, and measure any tool pitch against it before you buy anything.
runbook.verify: Read the top item on the list back to the department head who named it and ask, 'if we fixed only this and nothing else, would it matter?' If they hesitate, it isn't the real constraint — go back to the question.

tryThis.title: Map one process you've never written down
tryThis.setup: Pick a process you personally run every week that nobody else at the station could run tomorrow. Open Claude Code in a folder where you can save a file. Have 30 uninterrupted minutes — this is an interview, not a prompt.
tryThis.prompt:
I run [the weekly underwriting spot-copy review] at a public radio station and it exists nowhere except in my head. I want you to interview me and turn it into a written process document that a new hire could actually follow.

Rules for you:
- Ask me ONE question at a time and wait for my answer. Do not write the document until I say we're done.
- Start with what triggers this process — what event kicks it off, and how often does it happen?
- Then walk me step by step through what I actually do. When I skip a step because it's obvious to me, catch it and ask.
- Push me hard on the judgment calls: the places where I decide something based on experience rather than a rule. Those are the parts that will be lost if I leave, so make me say them out loud.
- Ask what has gone wrong in the past and what I now do to prevent it.

When I say we're done, write it to PROCESS.md with these sections: Trigger, Inputs, Steps, Judgment calls and why, Failure modes, Who to ask when it breaks. Flag anywhere I was vague — don't smooth it over or guess.

Don't include any donor names, contact info, or giving history in the document. Refer to systems and roles, not people's data.

pitfalls: Waiting for auto-compact to save you. The video is clear that it kicks in too late — by then the answers have already gotten worse. Hand off deliberately at around a quarter of the window, not when the machine forces it.
Running /clear before you've written the handoff. That is genuinely gone. Get the summary out first, copy it, then clear.
Assuming a fresh session is empty. Your CLAUDE.md, skills, and memory files load automatically — a brand-new session may already be 30-40k tokens deep before you type a word.
Automating a broken process. If the traffic log workflow is a mess, automating it gives you a faster mess. Fix the step, then speed it up.

Building with no number. Before you start, name the one metric that would make this worth having done. Without it, nobody can tell six months later whether it worked, and the next AI project gets killed.

keyQuotes.quote: Answer quality drifts as the context window fills. It causes Claude to get confused, forget things — that is called the dumb zone. It's also called context rot.
If you don't clearly understand the process already, then how in the world do you expect an intern to understand it, or an AI agent to understand it?
Automate a broken process and you don't fix it — you scale it. You can't automate what you can't map.

modules.title: Delegation: Freelancers, and a Newsroom
modules.kicker: Sub-agents, agent teams, and knowing when not to

sourceChapters.title: Building a Sub-Agent: The Plan Roaster

modules.plainSummary: Up to now you have been working with one AI in one conversation. This module is about running more than one. A sub-agent is a worker your main session hands a job to — it gets a fresh, empty head, does the work in its own conversation, and reports back one paragraph instead of dumping 300 pages into yours. You build one by writing a single markdown file: a name, a description that says when to trigger it, which tools it may touch, and which model it runs on. An agent team is different and more expensive: several agents that can actually talk to each other, share a task list, and argue toward a consensus. The last third of the module is the part most people skip — when NOT to delegate.
modules.whyItMatters: A station of nine people cannot afford a research assistant, a compliance reader, and a devil's advocate. Sub-agents are the closest thing: you write the brief once and reuse it forever, and the cheap ones do the reading so your expensive main session stays clear. Just as important, the tool-restriction feature here is the only honest way to let AI near sensitive material — a read-only sub-agent physically cannot write or send anything, which is a real control, unlike asking it politely not to.

concepts.plain: A worker your main AI session hands a job to. It starts with an empty head, does the work in its own separate conversation, and reports back a short summary. It can only talk to your main session, never to you directly.
concepts.stationTranslation: A freelancer you brief once and reuse. You hand them the assignment, they go work, they file. They don't sit in on your other meetings.

concepts.term: Why delegate: a clean context window
concepts.plain: The sub-agent's reading fills up its own memory, not yours. In the demo, a roasting sub-agent burned 22,800 tokens of reading and thinking, but the main session only received the finished verdict.
concepts.stationTranslation: The intern reads the whole 300-page CPB filing. You get the one-page memo. Your desk stays clear for the show.

concepts.plain: One question decides it: is this about to dump a pile of stuff into my chat that I'll never read again? Yes means delegate. Other signals: lots of files to read, a wall of output, a job you keep repeating, independent work that can run in parallel, or a review you want from someone with no memory of your earlier arguing.
concepts.stationTranslation: If it's a stack of documents nobody will reread, send it out. If it's a two-line edit to your newsletter, just do it yourself.

concepts.plain: A sub-agent is one markdown file. The top section — the front matter — holds its name, description, allowed tools, and model. Claude only reads that top section to decide whether to call the agent, so a vague description means it fires when you don't want it and stays silent when you do. Those are misfires, and you fix them by rewriting the description.
concepts.stationTranslation: It's the line in your staff directory that says what this person does. Write 'handles stuff' and nobody routes work to them.

concepts.plain: Each sub-agent can run on a different model. Your main session can be the expensive one while the workers doing bulk reading run on Haiku, the cheapest. Reading a long report to pull three facts does not need the most expensive model.
concepts.stationTranslation: Your news director doesn't transcribe the school board tape. An intern does, and the director reads the summary.

concepts.term: Read-only by tool restriction, not by asking
concepts.plain: When you build a sub-agent you choose exactly which tools it may use. Pick read-only and it physically cannot write files, edit, or send anything. The video is blunt: if your AI could read data, assume it will — so a permission layer is a real control and a polite instruction in a prompt is not.
concepts.stationTranslation: The difference between telling a contractor 'please don't go in the donor files' and simply not giving them that key.

concepts.plain: Different from sub-agents. The main agent spins up a team that shares one task list, and the teammates can talk to each other, claim tasks, and debate to a consensus before reporting back. Sub-agents work alone in silence; a team works in a room. Teams cost noticeably more, and the feature is experimental — you switch it on in settings and you must say 'team create' or Claude will just quietly spin up ordinary sub-agents instead.
concepts.stationTranslation: Sub-agents are five freelancers in five cubicles. An agent team is an editorial meeting — the same people arguing the same story out loud until they land it.

glossary.definition: A separate AI worker with a fresh, empty memory, given one job by your main session. Reports back a summary. Defined in one markdown file.
Several agents that share a task list and can talk to each other, debate, and reach consensus. More expensive than sub-agents. Experimental feature.
Your main session. The only one you talk to. It hands work out to sub-agents and relays their findings back to you.
The settings block at the top of a sub-agent's markdown file: name, description, allowed tools, model, memory. Everything below it is the instructions.
Claude reads only a sub-agent's name and description to decide whether to use it, so it doesn't waste effort loading agents it won't need.
A sub-agent fires when you didn't want it, or stays silent when you did. Fixed by rewriting its description, not its instructions.
Claude fans a big job out to many sub-agents at once — sometimes dozens. Powerful, but it eats your usage limit fast.
Your rolling five-hour usage cap. Sub-agents and agent teams don't fill your chat, but they absolutely spend against this.

useCases.title: The Compliance Roaster
useCases.scenario: A sponsor sends back copy with 'the best rates in Milwaukee — call today!' baked in, and you have twenty spots to clear before the log closes.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Build a read-only sub-agent whose only job is to be hostile to your spot copy: it hunts for calls to action, price claims, and qualitative language, and files a line-by-line teardown with the exact phrase flagged. Because it starts fresh every time, it has no memory of the copy you argued for last week. You point it at your station's underwriting policy doc so it roasts against your rules, not a generic idea of the FCC.
useCases.timeSaved: A 90-minute copy-clearing pass becomes a 15-minute review of what got flagged
useCases.guardrail: The roaster flags, it does not approve. Nothing airs on a sub-agent's say-so — a human who is accountable for the license signs off, every time.
useCases.moduleTitle: Delegation: Freelancers, and a Newsroom

steps: Put your station's underwriting policy in a plain text or markdown file inside a project folder, plus the spot copy you're allowed to share.
In Claude Code, type /agents, choose Create new agent, then Project level, then Generate with Claude.
When it asks about tools, choose read-only — this sub-agent should never be able to write or send anything.
Describe the job in plain language: hunt for calls to action, price claims, and promotional language, and check the copy against your station's policy file, not a generic idea of the FCC rules.
Tell it what to hand back: a line-by-line list of the exact flagged phrase, why it's a problem, and a compliant rewrite, ending in a verdict of CLEAR, NEEDS EDIT, or REWRITE.
Tell it explicitly it must never say copy is approved — only a human clears copy for air.
Run it against a real piece of spot copy and read every line it flags before you touch anything.
Have a person accountable for the license make the actual clearance decision — the sub-agent's verdict is input, not sign-off.
runbook.verify: Hand it copy you already know has a problem — a stray 'call today' or a 'best rates' claim — and confirm it actually gets flagged. If the sub-agent calls known-bad copy CLEAR, its description or policy file needs fixing before you trust it on real spots.

useCases.title: Pitch Roaster before the morning meeting
useCases.scenario: A reporter pitches a three-week investigation. Everyone in the room likes it, which is exactly the problem.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: The video's own reason for building a roaster is that AI defaults to being a yes-man, so you build one agent whose job is to disagree. Hand it the pitch and it comes back with the holes: what's the actual accountability question, who is the missing voice, what happens if the central document doesn't exist, what's the cheaper version of this story. A fresh sub-agent has no stake in the newsroom's enthusiasm.
useCases.timeSaved: Kills a doomed three-week story in an afternoon instead of week two
useCases.guardrail: Pitch framing only. No source names, no unpublished confidential material, no tips from people who haven't agreed to be identified — a pitch memo can be stripped down to the question without them.
useCases.moduleTitle: Delegation: Freelancers, and a Newsroom

steps: Strip the pitch down to the question and the plan before it goes anywhere near Claude — no source names, no unpublished material, no tips from anyone who hasn't agreed to be identified.
In Claude Code, type /agents and create a new project-level sub-agent, read-only tools.
Describe its job as disagreeing on purpose: find the accountability question, the missing voice, what happens if the central document doesn't exist, and the cheaper version of the story.
Tell it explicitly not to be encouraging — its only job is to find the holes the room is too excited to see.
Hand it the stripped-down pitch and let it file its objections before your morning meeting.
Read every objection and decide, story by story, whether it's a real hole or a question you can already answer.
Bring the surviving pitches — and the holes the sub-agent found in the ones that didn't survive — into the room.
runbook.verify: Pick a pitch everyone already loves and run it through the sub-agent first. If it comes back with zero objections on a story that hasn't been reported yet, the agent isn't actually pushing back — rewrite its description to be more adversarial.

useCases.title: Forty local submissions, forty briefs
useCases.scenario: The local music inbox has forty new submissions and your music director has one afternoon.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: This is the parallel-and-independent case the video calls out: each submission is unrelated to the others and order doesn't matter, so you fan them out to cheap sub-agents. Each one reads the bio, the press notes, and the links, and files a one-paragraph brief — who they are, where they're from, what it sounds like on paper, whether they're actually local. Your director gets forty briefs instead of forty inboxes.
useCases.timeSaved: A full afternoon of triage becomes about 45 minutes of listening to the shortlist
useCases.guardrail: No song gets added to rotation because an AI summarized it well. A human listens to every track that advances — the briefs only decide listening order.
useCases.moduleTitle: Delegation: Freelancers, and a Newsroom

steps: Gather the forty submissions — bio, press notes, and links — into a folder called station-work.
In Claude Code, ask it to fan the batch out to sub-agents, one submission per sub-agent, since each one is independent and order doesn't matter.
Tell it what each brief should cover: who the artist is, where they're from, what it sounds like on paper, and whether they're actually local.
Run cheap sub-agents for this — it's bulk reading for a short summary, not work that needs your most expensive model.
Collect the forty one-paragraph briefs back in your main session.
Read the briefs yourself and sort them into a shortlist by what sounds most promising.
Listen to every track on the shortlist yourself before anything moves toward rotation.
runbook.verify: Pick two or three submissions at random and read the original bio and press notes against the brief a sub-agent wrote. If the brief invents a fact that isn't in the source material, don't trust that brief's shortlist ranking without a second look.

useCases.title: Roast the drive plan, not the donors
useCases.scenario: You have a spring drive plan — goal, day parts, premium ladder, script themes — and three weeks to fix whatever's wrong with it.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Hand the plan document to a Plan Roaster sub-agent and tell it to play devil's advocate: where is the goal unsupported by last year's actual pace, which premium tier makes no economic sense, which ask is doing double duty. This is exactly the ice-cream-stand demo from the video, aimed at something real. Build it read-only so it cannot touch a single file you didn't hand it.
useCases.timeSaved: Replaces a planning retreat you were never going to schedule
useCases.guardrail: The plan document only. No CRM export, no donor names, no giving history, no lapsed-donor lists — not into the main session and not into a sub-agent. Aggregate numbers you typed yourself are fine; a file from the donor database is not.
useCases.moduleTitle: Delegation: Freelancers, and a Newsroom

steps: Confirm the plan document has no CRM export, donor names, giving history, or lapsed-donor lists in it — aggregate numbers you typed yourself are fine, a file pulled from the donor database is not.
In Claude Code, type /agents and create a project-level sub-agent with read-only tools, so it can't touch any file you didn't hand it.
Describe its job as playing devil's advocate on a spring drive plan: is the goal supported by last year's actual pace, does every premium tier make economic sense, is any ask doing double duty.
Hand it the plan document — goal, day parts, premium ladder, script themes.
Let it come back with a written teardown of where the plan doesn't hold up.
Go through the teardown with your team and decide, item by item, what's a real problem versus a thing you can defend.
Revise the plan document based only on what you've decided is a real problem.
runbook.verify: Open the plan document you handed it and search for any donor name, dollar figure tied to a person, or giving history before you send it anywhere. If you find one, the plan document wasn't ready to hand to a sub-agent in the first place.

useCases.title: An agent team on the CPB narrative
useCases.scenario: Your Local Content and Service Report narrative is drafted and it reads like it was written by the person who wanted it to be true.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: This is the case for a real agent team rather than lone sub-agents — you want the perspectives to argue with each other. Spin up a team with distinct seats: a skeptical grant reviewer, a community member who never listens to you, your GM, a station down the road doing it better. They debate the narrative across a couple of rounds and hand back a consensus on what's actually persuasive and what's filler. Remember to say 'team create' or Claude will quietly give you silent sub-agents instead.
useCases.timeSaved: Two rounds of internal review compressed into one working session
useCases.guardrail: Every number in the narrative is verified by a human against your own records. An agent team will happily reach a confident consensus about a statistic it invented.
useCases.moduleTitle: Delegation: Freelancers, and a Newsroom

steps: Have your drafted LCSR narrative ready in a file, with every number in it already sourced back to your own records.
In Claude Code, turn on agent teams in settings if it isn't already on — this feature is experimental.
Say the words 'team create' out loud in your prompt — otherwise Claude will quietly hand you ordinary sub-agents instead of a team.
Give the team distinct seats: a skeptical grant reviewer, a community member who doesn't already listen to your station, your GM, and a station down the road doing this well.
Hand the team your narrative and let them debate it across a couple of rounds until they reach a consensus.
Read back what they agreed was persuasive and what they agreed was filler.
Revise the narrative using their consensus, but verify every number in the revised version against your own records yourself.
Watch your usage as you go — a team that talks to itself for multiple rounds spends against your five-hour session limit fast.
runbook.verify: Take every statistic the team's consensus praised as persuasive and trace it back to your own records by hand. An agent team will reach a confident consensus about a number it invented just as easily as one that's real.

tryThis.title: Build a Compliance Roaster
tryThis.setup: Have your station's underwriting policy in a plain text or markdown file inside your project folder, plus one piece of real spot copy you're allowed to share. Then type /agents, choose 'Create new agent', pick Project level, pick 'Generate with Claude', and when it asks about tools choose read-only.
tryThis.prompt: Create me a sub-agent called compliance-roaster. Its job is to be adversarial about noncommercial underwriting copy for a public radio station. When I hand it spot copy, it should NOT tell me it looks good. It should hunt for every FCC problem: calls to action ('call today', 'visit now', 'don't miss'), price and value information, comparative or qualitative claims ('best', 'leading', 'award-winning' used promotionally), and anything that reads like an ad instead of an identification of a sponsor. It should read underwriting-policy.md in this project and roast against OUR rules, not generic ones. Output: a line-by-line table with the exact offending phrase, why it's a problem, and a compliant rewrite. End with a verdict: CLEAR, NEEDS EDIT, or REWRITE. It must never say copy is approved — only a human clears copy. Trigger it whenever I say 'roast this copy', 'check this spot', or 'compliance check'. Read-only tools, Haiku model.

pitfalls: Don't force it. The video is explicit: sub-agents are a great feature, but forcing too many of them gives worse results. A quick edit, or steps that depend on each other in order, belong in your main session.

Sub-agents can't talk to you or each other. If the work needs to ask you a clarifying question, or needs the full history of what you two have been discussing, keep it in the main session — the sub-agent wakes up blind and only reports back to the orchestrator.

The description is where it breaks. A vague description means misfires, and a single unclosed quote in the front matter silently stops the agent from ever firing — that exact mistake happens on camera. If it didn't run, ask Claude to read its own agent file and tell you why.

Teams and big fan-outs eat your usage limit. Agent teams talk, and talking costs tokens; one dynamic workflow in the video spun up 210 agents and burned through the session limit. It won't clog your chat window — it will end your afternoon.

Borrowed agents are code you didn't write. Grabbing someone's sub-agent markdown off GitHub is easy, and the video warns to check it for prompt injection first. For a station: never install a borrowed agent that has write or send permissions anywhere near membership, traffic, or automation systems.

keyQuotes.quote: Is this about to dump a pile of stuff into my chat that I'll never read again? If that's ever yes, delegate it to a sub-agent. If it's no, then maybe you keep it in line.
Just because this feature is awesome, that doesn't mean to force it. Sometimes if you're forcing too many sub-agents, you're going to get worse results.
In agent teams, the main agent spins up the team and there's a shared task list. These specialized teammates can communicate between each other and claim tasks off the list.

modules.title: Build It, Ship It, and Know When to Trust It
modules.kicker: From a blank folder to a live URL

sourceChapters.title: The AI Systems Pyramid & Trusting the Output

modules.plainSummary: This module is where the abstract becomes a thing on the internet. First you build a web page — a pledge microsite, an election guide, a local music showcase — by giving Claude Code a folder of your brand assets and a one-sentence prompt, then letting it screenshot its own work and fix what looks wrong. You can point it at a site you admire and have it build something in that spirit, then swap in your colors, your logo, your copy. Then you put it online: GitHub stores the files, Vercel turns them into a real address people can visit, and the two talk to each other so a change you approve goes live in about a minute. The last and most important part is the AI Systems Pyramid — a four-level map of how much freedom you are handing the machine, and the hard rule that a prompt is a suggestion, not a lock.
modules.whyItMatters: The microsite your station keeps postponing — the one that needs a $12,000 agency invoice or a six-week wait for the one web person — is now a weekend. But the same power that builds a pledge page in an afternoon can also send 150,000 emails you never asked for, and this module is the only one that tells you where the brakes are.

concepts.plain: A free add-on you install once. It tells Claude how to make pages that look professionally designed instead of obviously machine-made — real spacing, real type, real motion.
concepts.stationTranslation: The difference between a page that looks like your station and a page that looks like a 2009 WordPress template someone abandoned.

concepts.plain: Claude builds the page, opens it in a browser, takes a picture of it, looks at the picture, and fixes what looks wrong — twice — before it shows you anything.
concepts.stationTranslation: It listens back to its own tape before airing it. Turn it off for animated backgrounds — a still photo of motion confuses it into endless re-editing.

concepts.plain: Take a full-page screenshot of a site you like, hand it to Claude with the page's style code, and it builds something structurally similar that you then repaint with your own brand.
concepts.stationTranslation: Like a producer saying 'give me a show that feels like Radiolab.' You're borrowing the structure and pacing, not the content — then making it yours.

concepts.plain: GitHub is a cloud filing cabinet for your project that remembers every version. Vercel is the thing that takes what's in that cabinet and turns it into a web address anyone can visit.
concepts.stationTranslation: GitHub is the archive with every cut of the piece. Vercel is the transmitter. Approve a change, it goes to the archive, and the transmitter picks it up automatically.

concepts.plain: 'Local host' is a private preview only on your machine. Nothing reaches the public site until you explicitly say push. Write that rule into your CLAUDE.md so it never guesses.
concepts.stationTranslation: The production studio versus the air chain. Everything gets built and checked off-air. You say the word, and only then does it hit the transmitter.

concepts.plain: Deterministic means same input, same output, every time — a vending machine. Non-deterministic means the AI generates something fresh each run. Flexible, but you cannot predict it exactly.
concepts.stationTranslation: A carted promo plays identically every time. A live host reading the same copy will do it differently each hour. Both are fine — you just have to know which one you built.

concepts.term: The tool layer beats the prompt layer
concepts.plain: Telling an AI 'never send an email' works 100 times and fails on the 101st. Removing the send-email tool entirely means it cannot send one even if it tries.
concepts.stationTranslation: Don't post a sign saying 'do not key the mic.' Unplug the mic. Give it a draft tool and no send tool, a read tool and no delete tool.

glossary.definition: A cloud home for your project's files that keeps every past version, so any change can be traced or undone.
A service that takes the files in your GitHub repo and publishes them as a live website at a real web address.
Moving your work from a private preview on your own computer to a public address the world can open.
One project's folder on GitHub — all the files plus the full history of who changed what and when.
A private preview of your site that runs only on your own machine. Nobody else can see it.
A folder with your logo and brand guidelines that you point Claude at, so what it builds comes out in your colors and type.
A set of known inputs with known correct outputs. Run them against your automation to see how often it gets it right before you trust it.
A list of files GitHub should never upload — API keys, passwords, anything sensitive. Check it before your first push.

useCases.title: The pledge-drive microsite you never had time for
useCases.scenario: Fall drive is three weeks out. You want a single page with the case for support, the thank-you gifts, a challenge-grant thermometer, and a big button to the donation form — and the web person is booked through November.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Drop your logo and brand guidelines into a brand_assets folder, describe the page in a sentence, and let the front-end design skill and the screenshot loop build and self-correct it. Push it to GitHub, import it into Vercel, and it's live on a real URL. Copy edits after that take a minute each.
useCases.timeSaved: A $12k agency invoice and a six-week queue become an afternoon and a $20 domain
useCases.guardrail: The donate button must point to your existing, PCI-compliant donation platform. Do not let Claude build a form that collects card numbers, names, or addresses. The page links out; it never captures donor data.
useCases.moduleTitle: Build It, Ship It, and Know When to Trust It

steps: Make a project folder on your computer, and inside it a folder called brand_assets — drop in your logo and anything showing your colors and fonts.

Open Claude Code in that project folder and describe the page in one sentence: a hero, a case-for-support section, thank-you gifts, a progress thermometer, and a big donate button — tell it to match brand_assets exactly.
Tell it the donate button must only point to your station's existing donation platform — it must never build a form that collects a name, email, or card number.
Have it check its own work in a phone-width browser view and fix anything that looks off, twice, before it shows you anything.
Review the local preview on your own machine — this step is private, nothing is public yet.

Once you approve it, have it push the files to GitHub (an online backup that keeps every version of your project) and import that into Vercel (the service that turns those files into a live web address).
Click the donate button on the live page all the way through to confirm it lands on your real donation form.
runbook.verify: Click the donate button on the live page and confirm it lands on your station's actual donation form — not a page Claude built that asks for a name, email, or card number.

useCases.title: An election guide that ships before the election
useCases.scenario: You have candidate questionnaires for 14 local races and no way to present them beyond a wall of PDFs nobody opens.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Give Claude your questionnaire responses as files and ask for a clean, searchable page — one card per race, candidates side by side, mobile-first. The screenshot loop makes it legible on a phone before you ever look at it. Deploy to Vercel; corrections push live in a minute.
useCases.timeSaved: Two weeks of web queue becomes about two days, most of it fact-checking
useCases.guardrail: Every candidate's words are verbatim from their questionnaire — Claude formats, it does not paraphrase or summarize a candidate's position. An editor reads every card against the source before it goes live.
useCases.moduleTitle: Build It, Ship It, and Know When to Trust It

steps: Gather each candidate's questionnaire responses as separate files, one per candidate, with the race clearly labeled.
Open Claude Code in a folder with all the files and ask for a clean, searchable page — one card per race, candidates side by side, built for a phone screen first.
Tell it to use each candidate's own words only — it formats the responses, it never paraphrases or summarizes a position.
Have it check the page at phone width and fix anything illegible before it shows you the result.
Have an editor read every single card against the original questionnaire, word for word.
Fix any mismatches, then have Claude push the files to GitHub and import them into Vercel — the service that turns the project into a live web address — so it's live.
Do one more read of the live page before you share the link publicly.
runbook.verify: Have an editor compare every published card, word for word, against the original questionnaire — if even one phrase doesn't match, pull the page down until it's fixed.

useCases.title: A local music showcase page for the station's featured artists
useCases.scenario: Your music director wants a page for the 20 local artists in rotation this quarter — photo, bio, embedded track, show dates — and it needs to look better than a spreadsheet.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Find a music site whose layout you like, screenshot it, and use it as a structural reference. Then have Claude rebuild it with your brand colors and your artists. Pull individual pieces — a nicer card, a better hover state — from a component library like 21st.dev and ask Claude to work them in.
useCases.timeSaved: A quarterly page refresh drops from a two-week ask to an afternoon
useCases.guardrail: Embed audio through the platforms that already hold the license (Bandcamp, Spotify, the artist's own player). Do not have Claude host or serve audio files directly — that is a licensing question, not a coding one.
useCases.moduleTitle: Build It, Ship It, and Know When to Trust It

steps: Find a music site whose layout you like and take a full-page screenshot of it.
Open Claude Code in a project folder and hand it that screenshot plus your artists' photos, bios, and show dates.
Ask it to rebuild that layout's structure using your brand colors, logo, and your artists instead of the original site's content.
Tell it to embed each track through Bandcamp, Spotify, or the artist's own player — it must never host or serve an audio file directly.
Optionally, pull a nicer card design or hover effect from a component library like 21st.dev and ask Claude to work it into the page.
Have it check the page at phone width and fix anything that looks wrong before it shows you the result.
Push the files to GitHub and import them into Vercel, and confirm the page is live at a real web address.
runbook.verify: Play every embedded track on the live page — if any artist's track doesn't load through their own streaming platform, the embed is broken or hosted the wrong way.

useCases.title: Deciding how much rope to give any AI project
useCases.scenario: Three departments now want to automate something. You have no framework for saying yes to one and not yet to another, and no one has been able to explain the actual risk to your board.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Use the AI Systems Pyramid as a governance tool. Ask of every proposal: which level is this — chatbot, workflow, AI workflow, or agent? Push each one down a level if you can. Most things a station wants are AI workflows wearing an agent costume, and the workflow version is cheaper, faster, and nearly failure-free.
useCases.timeSaved: Replaces months of vague AI-policy debate with one four-level question
useCases.guardrail: Any automation touching donor records, member data, or anything publishable stays at chatbot level — human-triggered, human-reviewed — until it has been run against a set of known cases and passed.
useCases.moduleTitle: Build It, Ship It, and Know When to Trust It

steps: List every department's automation request on one page, one line each.
For each request, ask which of the four levels it actually is: a chatbot you trigger yourself, a plain no-AI workflow, a fixed AI workflow, or a full agent.
For anything that looks like an agent, ask whether a fixed AI workflow could do the same job — if it can, approve the lower level, not the agent.
For anything touching donor records, member data, or anything publishable, require it stay at chatbot level — human-triggered, human-reviewed — until proven otherwise.
Write your decision and the level for each request in one shared document leadership can point back to.
Require any approved AI workflow or agent to be run against a set of known test cases and pass before it goes live.
runbook.verify: Pick one approved automation and ask its owner to list every tool it can actually touch — if that list includes anything the task doesn't need (sending, deleting, publishing), send it back before it launches.

useCases.title: Auditing the automations nobody remembers building
useCases.scenario: Someone set up an AI helper months ago. It still runs. Nobody is sure exactly what it can reach, and nobody has looked at its output in weeks.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Inventory every automation and, for each, list the tools it can actually touch — not what the prompt tells it to do, but what it is physically able to do. Strip out every tool it does not need: no send, no delete, no publish. Then ask Claude directly to help you enumerate the edge cases and design a safe failure for each one.
useCases.timeSaved: One afternoon now versus one very bad week later
useCases.guardrail: This is exactly how the instructor's own forgotten automation started DMing the wrong people and posting to public channels. If an automation can reach your CRM, your CMS, or your listener email list, it needs a hard tool-level wall — not a polite instruction.
useCases.moduleTitle: Build It, Ship It, and Know When to Trust It

steps: List every AI automation currently running at your station, including any nobody remembers setting up.
For each one, list every tool it can actually reach — not what its instructions say, what it is physically able to do: send, delete, post, read.
Strip out every tool it doesn't need for its actual job, especially any send, delete, or publish access it isn't using.
For anything that can reach your CRM, your website, or your listener email list, add a hard restriction at the tool level — not just a written instruction telling it not to.
Ask Claude to help you list the edge cases each automation could hit and what a safe failure looks like for each one.
Check each automation's actual output from the last two weeks against what it's supposed to be doing.
Put a date on the calendar to repeat this whole check, so a new automation can't quietly reappear unwatched.
runbook.verify: For each automation, look at the tools it still has and try to name one bad real-world action it could take with each — if you can name one, remove that tool now, don't wait for it to happen.

tryThis.title: Build and ship a real one-page site for something your station is doing next month
tryThis.setup: Make a folder for the project. Inside it, make another folder called brand_assets and drop in your station logo (any format) and your brand guidelines — a PDF, a screenshot of the style guide, even a note listing your hex colors and fonts. If you don't have a guideline doc, a paragraph in a text file works. You'll need a free GitHub account and a free Vercel account to publish it; sign into Vercel with your GitHub login and it wires itself together.
tryThis.prompt:
Build me a modern, professional one-page site for our fall pledge drive. Use @brand_assets/ for our logo, colors, and typography — match them exactly.

Sections, in order: a hero with our tagline and a big 'Become a Member' button; a short case-for-support section; a challenge-grant progress bar (hardcode it at 40% for now); a thank-you gift tier list with three tiers; and a footer with our call letters, address, and a link to our privacy policy.

Rules:
- Mobile-first. Most of our listeners will open this on a phone.
- The 'Become a Member' button links out to our existing donation platform. Do NOT build a form that collects any personal or payment information.
- Use the screenshot loop to check your own work at phone width before you show me anything.
- Keep everything on local host. Do not push anything to GitHub until I explicitly tell you to.

Show me the local preview when it's ready and I'll give you notes.

pitfalls: Prompting is not a permission layer. The instructor told an automation to DM only him; it obeyed for five weeks, then started posting stats into public channels — because it still had access to every channel. If it can touch a thing, assume it eventually will.

Bypass permissions mode ('dangerously skip permissions') lets Claude run any command without asking. The instructor uses it and admits it is dangerous. Fine for a throwaway design folder while you are watching. Never on a machine with credentials to your CRM, CMS, or automation system.

Check .gitignore before your first push. GitHub repos can be public, and API keys, passwords, or an exported member list committed by accident are effectively published. Ask Claude what it is about to upload before you say push.

A cloned site is a starting structure, not a finished product. The generated copy, stats, and testimonials are placeholder fiction — the instructor's demo page invented its own statistics. Every number and every quote on a station page must be real and checked before it goes live.

Don't build an agent when a workflow will do. The instructor's rule-based inbox workflow was more reliable, cheaper, faster, and easier to debug than the AI agent that did the same job — and had 'basically no failures.'

levels.name: Chatbots (and skills you invoke yourself)
levels.plain: A human triggers it, every time. You ask, it answers or does one thing, you take the output and decide what happens next. You are sitting there the whole time.
levels.stationExample: A producer asks Claude to summarize a 40-minute council meeting transcript and pull the three usable quotes. Nothing moves until the producer moves it.
levels.trustLevel: Lowest autonomy, lowest risk. Nothing happens without you. This is where almost everything a station does should start — and where most of it should stay.

levels.name: Workflows (deterministic, no AI)
levels.plain: Plain old automation, triggered by an event or a schedule, with no AI in it at all. Same input, same output, every single time. It runs while you sleep.
levels.stationExample: Every Monday at 6am, pull last week's stream numbers from the analytics export and drop them into a shared spreadsheet. No judgment involved, so no AI needed.
levels.trustLevel: Runs unattended, but fully predictable. Higher autonomy than a chatbot, and yet arguably safer — because you know exactly what it will do. If a job doesn't need AI, do not put AI in it.

levels.name: AI workflows
levels.plain: A fixed chain of steps — 1, 2, 3, 4 — where one of the steps is an AI step. You know the order and the path. You just don't know exactly what the AI step will produce.
levels.stationExample: An underwriting inquiry hits the inbox. The workflow looks up whether the sender is an existing sponsor (objective), has AI draft a reply (generative), and drops it in the AE's drafts folder. Same three steps, every time.
levels.trustLevel: Medium. The route is locked; only the wording varies. This is the sweet spot for most station automation — the instructor rebuilt his unreliable inbox agent as an AI workflow and it went to 'basically no failures,' cheaper and faster.

levels.name: Agents
levels.plain: You hand the AI a set of tools and a goal, and it decides which tools to use, in what order, how many times. You do not know the path in advance. That is the whole point of it.
levels.stationExample: A research agent that, given a story tip, decides on its own whether to search public records, pull past coverage, check a database, or all three in some order — and comes back with a brief.
levels.trustLevel: Highest autonomy, highest unpredictability, highest cost, highest risk. Worth it only when the path genuinely cannot be known in advance. Enforce limits at the tool layer, never in the prompt, and never point one at donor data, your CMS, or your email list.

pyramid.bigIdea: As you climb the pyramid, four things rise together: autonomy, unpredictability, cost, and risk. So build the simplest system that does the job, and no simpler — and no more complex. Most things people reach for an agent to do are really an AI workflow, and most AI workflows are really just workflows. Push every idea down a level and see if it still works. Then, wherever you land, enforce your limits by removing tools, not by writing rules — because a prompt is a suggestion and a missing tool is a wall. The instructor's version: putting an automation live and walking away is like putting a kid on a bike with a helmet and going inside for a nap. You hold the seat. You run alongside. You let go slowly.

keyQuotes.quote: Prompting is not a permission layer. The agent could follow instructions 100 times, but on the 101st time, maybe it just gets a little confused and it ignores that prompt.
You have to assume that if an agent can read or touch or use something, assume that it will. Assume worst case scenario.
You want to design the simplest solution for the job. If the process doesn't need AI, there's no reason you should put AI in there.

modules.title: The Station's Second Brain
modules.kicker: Turn what your station knows into something AI can use

sourceChapters.title: The 5 Levels of a Second Brain

modules.plainSummary: Every AI chat you have today is thrown away tomorrow. This module shows how to stop that: build a folder of plain text files that your AI reads before it answers, so the knowledge stacks up instead of evaporating. The video walks through Andrej Karpathy's 'LLM wiki' idea — you drop raw documents into a 'raw' folder, Claude Code reads them, and it writes them back out as organized, cross-linked notes with an index. Then it lays out five levels of second brain, from a single routing file up to an always-on system, and is blunt that higher is not better — pick the lowest level that solves an actual pain. It closes with the 'grill-me' skill: a prompt that interviews you relentlessly about something you know and writes the answers to a document, because the hard part is not the AI retrieving knowledge, it's getting the knowledge out of your head in the first place.
modules.whyItMatters: Your station's real institutional memory — why the spring drive script works, which underwriter never renews, how the transmitter site gets reset at 2am — lives in the heads of three or four long-tenured people, and some of them are retiring. Every AI tool you buy is only as good as the context you can hand it, and right now that context is unwritten. This module is the cheapest succession-planning tool in the building: a folder of markdown files and a skill that interviews people before they walk out the door.

concepts.plain: A folder of plain text files holding what you know, organized so an AI can find the right one and answer from it instead of guessing.
concepts.stationTranslation: The station shared drive, except an AI can actually navigate it. The real test: could Claude find last spring's pledge script, and could you?

concepts.term: The LLM wiki (raw in, wiki out)
concepts.plain: You put source documents in a 'raw' folder. The AI reads them and writes organized, linked notes into a 'wiki' folder, plus an index and a running log of what it did.
concepts.stationTranslation: Dump five years of pledge drive recaps and board minutes into 'raw.' Claude reads them and writes back a browsable set of notes with an index, no hand-filing.

concepts.plain: The file Claude reads first in a project. Beyond who you are, it says where things live: 'if you need X, look in this folder.' Without those rules, Claude won't go looking.
concepts.stationTranslation: The map taped to the front of the filing cabinet. 'Underwriting history is in this drawer. Music rotation logic is in that one.' No map, no find.

concepts.term: It's just markdown — no database needed
concepts.plain: No vector database, no embeddings, no infrastructure. Plain text files in folders. Cost is basically zero; you only pay for the tokens the AI reads.
concepts.stationTranslation: This is a station-budget technology. There is nothing to buy, nothing to license, and nothing that stops working when the person who set it up leaves.

concepts.plain: Decide how you'll ask for the knowledge before you decide how to store it. The shape of the question determines the shape of the file.
concepts.stationTranslation: If you'll ask 'summarize the March board meeting,' store one whole file per meeting. If you'll ask 'what's rule 17,' chopping it into searchable pieces makes sense.

concepts.plain: Ingest the evergreen stuff — decisions, processes, history. Don't ingest the churn — email threads, live CRM data. Give the AI permission to go fetch that live instead.
concepts.stationTranslation: Your style guide and last year's drive results belong in the brain. Today's donor records do not — the brain should know they exist and never hold a copy.

concepts.plain: A skill that interviews you relentlessly about one topic, one question at a time, writing every answer to a document as it goes. It stops when there are no gaps left.
concepts.stationTranslation: Sit your retiring chief engineer down for ninety minutes. The AI asks the questions you wouldn't know to ask, and writes the runbook nobody ever had time to write.

glossary.definition: Plain text with light formatting. Opens in any editor, readable in 20 years, needs no software to survive.
A table of contents the AI reads first, so it knows which note to open instead of reading everything.
A link from one note to another. Follow it to jump from 'spring drive 2024' to 'sustainer messaging.'
A free app that displays your markdown files and draws the links between them as a picture. Optional — the files work without it.
Finding notes by meaning rather than exact wording. Searching 'listener complaints' surfaces a note that says 'audience feedback.'
Storage that records how things relate — 'Jordan works at Acme,' 'Acme sponsors Morning Edition' — not just that they're linked.
Periodically asking the AI to audit its own notes for gaps, contradictions, and missing information.
A Claude Code setting where it writes and updates a memory file on its own as you work. Toggle it with /memory.

levels.name: CLAUDE.md + folders (routing)
levels.plain: One file that tells the AI who you are and where everything lives, plus a handful of plain folders. Answers: can I find it by an exact name or word?
levels.stationExample: A station-ops folder: style guide, underwriting compliance rules, a decisions log. CLAUDE.md says 'FCC underwriting language lives in /compliance — read it before drafting any spot copy.'
levels.payoff: You stop re-explaining the station to the AI every session. This is where everyone starts, and where most stations should stay.

levels.name: The LLM wiki (Karpathy-style)
levels.plain: A 'raw' folder where you dump source documents and a 'wiki' folder the AI writes into — organized notes, cross-links, an index, and a log. Answers: can I pull everything on one topic together?
levels.stationExample: Drop five years of pledge drive scripts, hourly results, and post-drive recaps into raw. Claude builds notes on sustainer conversion, matching-gift language, and which day parts overperform, all linked.
levels.payoff: Big. The video's own presenter sits his entire operation here. One user cut query token usage 95% moving 383 files into a wiki.

levels.name: Semantic search (vector database)
levels.plain: Notes are chopped into chunks and indexed by meaning, so you can search with different words than you wrote. Answers: can it find it when I don't remember the wording?
levels.stationExample: A big searchable pile of show transcripts and archives where 'housing affordability' surfaces a segment that only ever said 'rent burden.'
levels.payoff: Real, but narrow. Great for pulling one specific fact out of a huge pile. Bad at whole-document work — it returns chunks, so it can't reliably summarize a full meeting.

levels.name: Knowledge graph (relationship mapping)
levels.plain: Stores entities and how they relate — not just 'these two notes link,' but 'this person works at this company, which sponsors this show.' Answers: can I follow a chain from X back to A?
levels.stationExample: Underwriting: this account exec sold this client, who sponsored this show, whose PD left, and the account lapsed. Trace the chain and you find why revenue moved.
levels.payoff: Powerful for relationship-heavy work like a donor or sponsor CRM. The presenter says outright he does not use one day-to-day — his work isn't relationship-heavy enough to justify it.

levels.name: Always-on brain OS
levels.plain: The brain syncs and refreshes itself continuously — always ingesting, always updating, no human pressing go. Tools like GBrain sit here.
levels.stationExample: A system that pulls in every meeting recording, every newsletter, every log automatically, with nobody deciding what belongs.
levels.payoff: Questionable, and the video says so. The presenter deliberately does NOT run level 5 — he wants control over what gets ingested, because too much context does more damage than good.

useCases.title: Grill the chief engineer before he retires
useCases.scenario: Your chief engineer has run the transmitter site for 22 years. The generator start sequence, the STL failover, which breaker is mislabeled — none of it is written down. He retires in eight months.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Run the grill-me skill: 'Grill me about the transmitter site — every routine, every failure mode, every workaround.' Claude interviews him one question at a time and checkpoints every answer to a markdown file, so nothing is lost even in a two-hour session. It flags what he can't answer so you know who else to ask. The result goes into a level-1 second brain as your engineering runbook.
useCases.timeSaved: A runbook nobody has written in 22 years becomes a real document in two or three sessions
useCases.guardrail: Do not put transmitter access codes, site alarm passwords, or EAS credentials in the files. Write 'credential stored in the station password manager' and point to it.
useCases.moduleTitle: The Station's Second Brain

steps: Before you start, tell Claude: never write down access codes, alarm passwords, or EAS credentials — write "stored in the station password manager" instead and move on.
Open Claude Code with the grill-me skill (a set of instructions that has it interview someone, one question at a time, and save every answer as it goes) and sit down with your chief engineer.
Ask Claude to grill him about the transmitter site — every routine, every failure mode, every workaround.
Let Claude checkpoint each answer to a document as you go, so nothing is lost even in a two-hour session.
When Claude flags a question he can't answer, write down who else on staff might know it.
Read the finished document together and correct anything Claude got wrong or garbled.
Save it into your station's second brain folder as the engineering runbook, and point to it from CLAUDE.md — the file Claude reads first, that tells it where things live.
runbook.verify: Search the finished document for anything that looks like a code or password — if you find one, delete it and replace it with "stored in the password manager."

useCases.title: A pledge drive brain that remembers every drive
useCases.scenario: Every drive, you rebuild the script from scratch and half-remember what worked. The results spreadsheets, the hourly logs, and the post-mortems are scattered across three drives and one person's inbox.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Drop past drive scripts, hourly result exports (dollars and counts only, no donor records), and post-drive recaps into a 'raw' folder and have Claude build the wiki. Ask it 'what pitch language preceded our best sustainer hours across the last five drives?' and it reads the index, follows the links, and answers from your own history instead of generic best practices.
useCases.timeSaved: A week of digging through old drives becomes an afternoon of asking questions
useCases.guardrail: Aggregate numbers only. No donor names, addresses, giving histories, or CRM exports ever go into the raw folder — the whole folder is readable by the AI, so if it's in there, it's uploaded.
useCases.moduleTitle: The Station's Second Brain

steps: Export only aggregate numbers — dollars and counts per hour — from each drive; never export donor names, addresses, giving histories, or a CRM record.
Put those exports, along with past drive scripts and post-drive recaps, into a folder called raw.
Open Claude Code in that folder and ask it to read everything and write it back out as a linked set of notes in a wiki folder, with an index.
Check the index and a few notes to confirm nothing beyond aggregate numbers made it in.
Ask your real question — for example, "what pitch language preceded our best sustainer hours across the last five drives?" — and let Claude answer from the index and its links.
Check the answer against the original scripts and recaps before you use it in planning.
Add each new drive's results and recap into raw after the drive, so the wiki keeps growing.
runbook.verify: Search every file in raw for an @ sign, a name next to a dollar amount, or anything that looks like a donor record — if you find one, pull that file out before Claude ever reads the folder.

useCases.title: Write down the rotation logic that only the MD knows
useCases.scenario: Your music director carries the rotation rules in her head: separation between similar artists, how new local music gets added, which categories can't touch drive time, how a track graduates or gets rested.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Grill-me the music director about the rotation logic, then store the output as the music section of the station brain. Now a fill-in host or a new MD can ask 'why can't these two tracks air in the same hour?' and get the station's actual answer. Add the local-music library notes and the wiki starts linking artists, categories, and rules together.
useCases.timeSaved: Onboarding a new music director drops from months of osmosis to weeks
useCases.guardrail: AI does not pick the music. It documents the reasoning; the MD still makes every call, and licensing/reporting obligations to SoundExchange and the PROs stay a human, verified process.
useCases.moduleTitle: The Station's Second Brain

steps: Open Claude Code with the grill-me skill and sit down with your music director.
Ask Claude to grill her about the rotation logic — separation rules, how local music gets added, drive-time restrictions, how a track graduates or gets rested — one question at a time.
Let Claude checkpoint every answer to a document as you go.
Have her review the finished document and correct anything Claude oversimplified or got wrong.
Save the document as the music section of your station's second brain, and add your local-music library notes alongside it.
Update CLAUDE.md so it points to this file whenever someone asks a rotation question.
Tell your team the AI documents the reasoning only — the MD still makes every call, and SoundExchange and PRO reporting stay a verified human process.
runbook.verify: Give the finished document to a fill-in host and ask her to answer "why can't these two tracks air in the same hour" using only the document — if she can't answer it from the file, the logic isn't actually written down yet.

useCases.title: Stop rewriting the same CPB narrative every year
useCases.scenario: The Local Content and Service Report and every grant application ask variations of the same questions about community impact, partnerships, and service. Each year someone reconstructs the answers from memory and last year's PDF.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Build a wiki from past grant applications, CPB reports, community partner lists, and board minutes. Claude organizes them into linked notes — partners, initiatives, outcomes — with an index. When the next report opens, ask it to assemble the source material for each section, with links back to the original documents so every claim is traceable.
useCases.timeSaved: A three-week reporting scramble becomes about a week of drafting and verification
useCases.guardrail: Every number and every claim in a CPB filing gets verified against the primary source by a human before submission. The brain gathers evidence; it does not certify it.
useCases.moduleTitle: The Station's Second Brain

steps: Gather past grant applications, CPB reports, community partner lists, and board minutes into a folder called raw.
Open Claude Code in that folder and ask it to organize everything into linked notes — partners, initiatives, outcomes — with an index, in a wiki folder.
When the next report opens, ask Claude to assemble the source material for each section, with a link back to the original document for every claim.
Read every claim and every number in the draft against its linked source document.
Fix anything unverified or unsourced before it goes into the draft you'll submit.
Have a second person check the final draft against the wiki's links before submission.
Add this year's finished report back into raw so next year's wiki starts fuller.
runbook.verify: Pick five sentences at random from the drafted report and follow each one's link back to its source document — if any of the five doesn't check out, stop and re-verify the whole section.

useCases.title: Pick the lowest level that fixes an actual pain
useCases.scenario: A vendor is pitching your GM an AI knowledge platform with a knowledge graph, embeddings, and a monthly seat cost. The station has 40 documents that matter and no budget.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: The module's own answer: don't buy it yet. Level 1 is a CLAUDE.md and some folders. Level 2 is a folder of markdown the AI organizes itself. Neither needs a vendor, a database, or a subscription. Move up a level only when you feel a specific pain — the video's presenter runs his entire operation at level 2 and deliberately refuses level 5.
useCases.timeSaved: A five-figure annual platform spend avoided until you can prove you need it
useCases.guardrail: Whatever you build, it must stay portable — plain files you own, not a vendor's format. And write down who maintains it, or in a year it's another dead shared drive.
useCases.moduleTitle: The Station's Second Brain

steps: List the documents your station actually has that matter for institutional knowledge.
Before buying anything, ask whether a CLAUDE.md and a few plain folders (level 1) already answer most of your team's questions.
If that's not enough, try dropping the documents into a raw folder and asking Claude to build a wiki (level 2) — no vendor, no database, no subscription.
Only consider semantic search, a knowledge graph, or an always-on platform if you can name the specific pain level 1 or 2 didn't solve.
Whatever you build, keep it as plain files you own, not a vendor's proprietary format.
Name one person as the owner of the brain and set a date for when it gets reviewed and refreshed.
Bring the vendor's pitch back only if your own level-1 or level-2 attempt genuinely fails at something specific.
