runbook.verify: Ask three staff members to find last year's pledge drive script or the underwriting compliance rule using only what you built — if any of them can't find it in under a minute, the level you're at isn't solving the pain yet.

tryThis.title: Build the station brain, level 1, in twenty minutes
tryThis.setup: Make an empty folder called station-brain on your machine. Copy in three things you already have and don't mind an AI reading: your station style guide, your underwriting copy rules, and one past pledge drive script. No donor data, no personnel files, no passwords. Open Claude Code in that folder.
tryThis.prompt:
You are helping me build the first level of a second brain for a public radio station. Read the files in this folder. Then:

1. Create a CLAUDE.md that acts as a router. It should say what this station is, who works here and what they do, and — most importantly — WHERE things live: which folder holds the style guide, which holds underwriting compliance rules, which holds pledge drive history. Include a rule that you must read the underwriting compliance file before drafting any sponsor copy.

2. Create a /context folder with an about-the-station.md and a decisions.md (a running log of choices we make, with dates).

3. Create an index.md listing what's in here, so you can find things without reading every file.

Ask me questions one at a time about anything you need to know and can't find in the files — don't guess. Then show me what's missing: what would a new employee need that isn't written down anywhere?

pitfalls: Everything you put in the folder gets sent to Anthropic when the AI reads it. The video says this out loud. That means no donor records, no CRM exports, no personnel files, no credentials — ever.
Higher level is not better. The presenter deliberately sits at level 2 and refuses level 5. If you don't have a pain, don't add architecture.
Semantic search is not magic. It returns chunks, so 'summarize the March board meeting' can silently miss half the meeting. For whole-document questions, keep the whole document as one file.
The AI will not find what you didn't tell it about. If your CLAUDE.md doesn't say where the file lives, Claude won't go hunting for it — and you'll blame the AI for your missing routing.
Don't ingest the churn. Slack threads, emails, and live data go stale and become noise you have to prune every month. Ingest evergreen knowledge; give the brain a path to look up the live stuff.
A second brain nobody maintains is just a shared drive with extra steps. Name an owner and a refresh cadence before you build it.

keyQuotes.quote: Normal AI chats are ephemeral — the knowledge disappears after the conversation. This method makes knowledge compound like interest in a bank.
Sometimes it seems like the bigger problem is getting everything out of your brain into the system. So before you blame AI, take a look at your folders and files.
If I had six hours to chop down a tree, I would spend the first four sharpening the axe.

modules.title: Work That Happens While You Sleep
modules.plainSummary: Everything up to now has been you sitting at the keyboard, talking to Claude. This module is about the opposite: saving a prompt once and having it run on its own — every weekday at 5:30am, or every time a form gets submitted. Claude Code now has 'routines,' which run the prompt on Anthropic's servers, so your computer can be off and it still fires. The module also teaches the harder judgment call: a lot of the things you want automated are boring and predictable, and for those you don't want an AI agent at all — you want a plain script that does the same thing the same way every morning. The video walks the whole build end to end: a 6am research brief that searches the web, writes it up, and drops it in the owner's chat tool.
modules.whyItMatters: Stations run on recurring grunt work nobody has time for: scanning agendas, watching grant deadlines, tracking new businesses, checking what local artists released this week. This is the module where that work stops being a person's Monday and starts being a file that ran at 5am. But it's also the module where you learn which of those jobs should never be handed to an AI that runs unsupervised.

concepts.plain: A prompt you save once and Claude runs on its own, on Anthropic's servers. Your laptop can be closed. It reads a GitHub folder you point it at, does the work, and reports back.
concepts.stationTranslation: Like a recorded show that airs on a timer — you set it up once, and it goes out at 5am whether or not anyone is in the building.

concepts.plain: A routine has to work without asking you anything, because you're asleep. So the prompt must be complete: what to look at, what to produce, where to send it, what to do if something fails.
concepts.stationTranslation: It's a script for a fill-in host who can't call you. Anything you'd normally clarify mid-task has to be written down before it airs.

concepts.plain: A routine can fire on a schedule (minimum once an hour), from an API call another system makes, or from an event like a new push to a GitHub repo.
concepts.stationTranslation: Clock-based, like a scheduled newscast. Or event-based — someone rings the bell and it wakes up. Most station automations are clock-based.

concepts.plain: The routine can't see files on your machine, so your API keys can't come from a local file. You paste them into the routine's environment settings, where they're stored separately from your code.
concepts.stationTranslation: The automation runs in a rented room, not your office. Anything it needs — keys, logins — has to be handed to it at the door, deliberately.

concepts.plain: Each run clones your folder fresh, does the job, then deletes the copy. Nothing carries over. No saved logins, no browser cookies, no memory of yesterday unless you make it write notes down somewhere.
concepts.stationTranslation: Every morning a new temp shows up who has never been here. They get the handbook (your project folder) and nothing else.

concepts.term: Agentic vs. deterministic — when NOT to use a routine
concepts.plain: If the work is a straight line — search, summarize, send — you don't need an AI agent deciding what to do. Write a plain script that does the same steps every time. Cheaper, more predictable, easier to trust.
concepts.stationTranslation: The traffic log doesn't need judgment; it needs to be right. Save the agent for jobs where the next step genuinely depends on what it found.

concepts.plain: Polling means checking over and over: 'anything new? anything new?' A webhook is a doorbell — the other system tells you the moment something happens. Better for events like a form submission.
concepts.stationTranslation: Don't station someone at the front door checking every ten minutes. Put in a bell. Volunteer signup, tip line, contest entry — those are doorbells.

glossary.definition: A saved prompt Claude Code runs by itself on a schedule, on Anthropic's servers, with your machine off.
Same idea, but it runs on your own computer — so your machine has to be on and awake.
The old term for 'run this on a clock.' You don't need to learn the syntax; you can just say 'weekdays at 6am.'
A password or API key handed to a program at startup instead of written into its files. Keeps secrets out of shared code.
A doorbell. One system pings another the instant something happens, instead of the second system constantly checking.
A prebuilt link between Claude and a tool you already use — Slack, Gmail, Google Drive. You switch it on in settings.
Cheap hosting services that run a small script on a schedule or on a webhook. Pennies per run. Used in the video's build.
The record of every automated run. When one fails, you paste the failed run back to Claude and ask it why.

useCases.title: The 5:30am story-lead brief
useCases.scenario: Your news director spends the first ninety minutes of every day scanning city council agendas, the county board site, the police log, and three local outlets — just to find out what's worth chasing.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: A scheduled routine pulls the public agenda feeds and searches local coverage overnight, then posts a short brief to the newsroom Slack: what's on the docket, what changed since last week, and three flagged 'this looks like a story' items with links. Realistically: most city clerks now run Legistar/CivicClerk/Granicus portals that publish agendas as public pages or RSS, so this is buildable today. Police blotters and PDF-only packets are hit or miss — start with what has a real URL and add the messy sources later.
useCases.timeSaved: A 90-minute morning scan becomes a 10-minute read of a brief someone else wrote overnight
useCases.guardrail: The brief is a tip sheet, not reporting. Every lead gets verified against the primary document by a human before anyone calls a source or writes a word. Never let a routine post to a public-facing channel.
useCases.moduleTitle: Work That Happens While You Sleep

steps: Before scheduling anything, run it by hand once in a normal Claude Code chat: give it the real URLs for your city council agenda page, the county board page, and two or three local news sites, and ask it to write today's brief.
Look at what came back — which sources it actually reached, which ones it couldn't, and where it guessed instead of saying a source failed.

Rewrite the prompt so nothing is left for it to guess: name every source URL, say exactly what should be in the brief, and say what to do if a site won't load — nobody will be there to answer questions at 5:30am.
Turn that tested prompt into a scheduled routine — a saved prompt Claude runs on its own, on Anthropic's servers, timed to finish before the morning news meeting.
Point the routine at an internal newsroom channel only. Never let it post to anything public-facing.
Put any API keys the routine needs into the routine's own settings, not a file on your computer — it runs on separate servers that can't see your machine.
Let it run for a few mornings unattended, and read every brief yourself before the newsroom does.
runbook.verify: Pick one flagged lead from the brief and open the actual agenda page or article it points to. If the link is dead, the date is wrong, or the item isn't really there, don't trust that morning's brief until you find out why.

useCases.title: Weekly new-business prospect scan
useCases.scenario: Your two-person corporate support team finds out a new restaurant opened six months after it opened — and after a commercial station already signed them.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: A Monday-morning routine checks the city/county new business license postings, the local business journal, and grand-opening coverage, then drops a ranked list into the underwriting team's shared doc: business name, what they do, contact info if it's public, why they might fit. This is a straight-line job — search, filter, format — so the video's advice applies: it may not even need the agentic version, just a script that runs every Monday.
useCases.timeSaved: A prospecting afternoon nobody ever actually does becomes a list waiting on Monday
useCases.guardrail: It's a lead list, not a pitch. No auto-emailing prospects, ever. And nothing about existing underwriters or their rates goes into the prompt.
useCases.moduleTitle: Work That Happens While You Sleep

steps: Decide first whether this even needs an AI agent — checking business listings and formatting a list is a straight-line job, so a plain scheduled script may do the whole thing for less money and less risk.
If you do want Claude Code involved, test it by hand first: point it at the city or county new-business license page and the local business journal, and ask for a draft prospect list.
Ask it to rank the list and format each entry the same way: business name, what they do, public contact info if available, and why they might fit as an underwriter.
Keep current underwriters and their rates completely out of the prompt — that information has no reason to be typed in at all.
Turn the tested prompt into a routine scheduled for Monday mornings, writing into the underwriting team's shared document only.
Put any API keys the routine needs into the routine's own settings, not a file on your computer.
Treat the weekly list as leads for a human to review, never as something that emails or contacts a prospect on its own.
runbook.verify: Look up the top-ranked business on the list yourself — it should be a real, currently open business at the address given. If it's closed, isn't real, or the contact info is wrong, the routine is reading stale or bad pages and needs fixing before you trust the rest.

useCases.title: Grant deadline radar
useCases.scenario: Someone forwards you a CPB or state arts board RFP eleven days before it closes, and the grant writer has to decide in an afternoon whether to even try.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: A weekly routine watches the funders you actually apply to — Grants.gov (which has a real public API), CPB, NEA, your state arts board, the two community foundations that fund you — and emails the development director a one-page radar: new opportunities, eligibility in one line, deadline, and a countdown on anything already tracked. Community foundation sites are usually plain web pages, so a web search step covers what has no feed.
useCases.timeSaved: Two hours a week of tab-checking, replaced by a page that arrives Monday — and one fewer missed deadline a year
useCases.guardrail: Eligibility and deadlines get confirmed against the actual RFP PDF before anyone starts writing. A hallucinated deadline that costs you a submission is the worst possible outcome here.
useCases.moduleTitle: Work That Happens While You Sleep

steps: List the exact funders to watch, with real web addresses for each — Grants.gov, CPB, NEA, your state arts board, and the specific community foundations you actually apply to.
Test it by hand once: ask Claude Code to check those sources today and draft the one-page radar — new opportunities, one line on eligibility, deadline, and a countdown on anything already tracked.
Tell it plainly what to do when a site won't load or a deadline isn't clearly posted: flag it as unclear, never guess.
Turn the tested prompt into a routine that runs weekly, with the radar emailed to the development director only.
Put any login or API keys the routine needs into the routine's own settings, not a file on your computer.
Before anyone acts on a deadline in the radar, open the actual RFP document yourself and confirm the date and eligibility line.
Review the radar every week it arrives — a missed week doesn't get skipped and caught up later.
runbook.verify: Pick one deadline from this week's radar and open the source RFP PDF directly. The date and eligibility line must match exactly — a hallucinated deadline that costs you a submission is the one failure this can't survive.

useCases.title: Local music radar for the adds meeting
useCases.scenario: Your music director wants the Tuesday adds meeting to start with the local scene, but assembling it means an hour of Bandcamp, venue calendars, and Instagram.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: A Monday-night routine scans new releases from artists on your local list, venue calendars for the next two weeks, and local music press, then writes the meeting prep doc: new local releases with links, who's touring through, and anything with a hook (album release show, hometown return). It writes into a shared file the MD opens Tuesday morning.
useCases.timeSaved: An hour of scattered tab-hunting becomes a prep doc already sitting there
useCases.guardrail: Adds are a human editorial call — the routine gathers, it doesn't recommend rotation. And it can't hear the record. Nothing gets added that a human hasn't listened to.
useCases.moduleTitle: Work That Happens While You Sleep

steps: Build your local artist list and your venue calendar links by hand first — the routine can only scan what you actually point it at.
Test it manually in Claude Code before scheduling anything: ask it to check new releases from your list, venue calendars for the next two weeks, and local music press, and write the meeting prep doc.
Tell it clearly that it only gathers and flags — it never recommends what should get added, that's the music director's call in the meeting.
Once a manual run looks right, turn it into a Monday-night routine that writes into the same shared file the music director opens Tuesday morning.
Keep the output to links and short context only — new releases, who's touring through, anything with a hook — with no ranking of what should be added.
Every Tuesday, make sure a human actually listens to anything before it goes into rotation — nothing gets added on the routine's say-so.
runbook.verify: Open two or three links from Tuesday's prep doc — they should land on the real release or the real calendar listing, not a paraphrase of one. If a link doesn't resolve to the actual source, don't trust that entry going into the meeting.

useCases.title: The churn brief that never touches the cloud
useCases.scenario: Leadership wants a monthly read on lapsed members — who's slipping away and what the pattern is — but that data is your donor CRM, and donor data does not go into a cloud AI.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: This is the case where the honest answer is 'not a cloud routine.' Because a routine runs on someone else's servers and can only see what you upload, the right build is a local scheduled task on a station machine: a script aggregates the CRM export into counts and cohorts — no names, no addresses, no giving history — and only those aggregate numbers are ever summarized. The video's own rule applies: if the automation can't reach it, it can't leak it.
useCases.timeSaved: A half-day monthly report becomes a 20-minute review of a draft
useCases.guardrail: Absolutely no donor PII leaves the building. Not names, not emails, not gift amounts tied to a person. Aggregate counts only, and a human reads the brief before leadership sees it.
useCases.moduleTitle: Work That Happens While You Sleep

steps: Export your CRM data and immediately strip it down to aggregate counts and cohorts only — no names, no emails, no addresses, no gift amounts tied to a person.

Save that stripped, aggregate-only file in a folder on your own machine. This job never goes near a cloud routine — a routine runs on someone else's servers, and it can only be trusted with what you're willing to upload.
Set up a local scheduled task on a station computer, not a cloud routine, to run the aggregation on a monthly schedule.
Ask Claude Code to summarize only the aggregate counts and cohort trends into a plain-English brief — never the underlying export.
Read the draft brief yourself and confirm every number in it traces back to the aggregate file, not to anything more detailed.
Only after your review does the brief go to leadership.
runbook.verify: Open the aggregate file the brief was built from and search it for an @ sign or anything that looks like a name. If anything personally identifying survived the strip, stop and fix the export before this runs again.

automationBlueprint.name: Newsroom Morning Story-Lead Brief
automationBlueprint.trigger: Cloud routine, weekdays at 5:30am station time (routines run at minimum hourly intervals, so a daily 5:30am fire is well within limits — and at 5 runs/day on Pro, one weekday brief is affordable)
steps: Pull the current and upcoming agendas from the city council and county board portals. Most of these run on Legistar, CivicClerk, or Granicus and expose public agenda pages or RSS — put the exact URLs in the project's CLAUDE.md so the routine doesn't have to guess.

Diff against yesterday: flag agenda items that are new, that moved to a vote, or that name a person or organization already on the newsroom's watch list (keep that list as a plain text file in the repo).
Run a web search over the last 24 hours of local outlets — the daily paper, the alt-weekly, the TV stations, the neighborhood blogs — for stories the newsroom hasn't already covered.

Check the public safety and public records feeds that actually publish machine-readable data. Be honest: many police blotters are scanned PDFs or a Facebook post. Skip what isn't reachable rather than faking it.

Write the brief: 5-8 items, each with a one-line what-happened, a link to the primary source, and a one-line 'why this might be a story.' Explicitly instruct it to say 'nothing notable in this category today' rather than padding.
Post to the newsroom Slack channel via the Slack connector, and also write the same brief as a dated markdown file in the repo so there's a searchable archive.
If any step fails, post a short failure note to Slack instead of silently doing nothing.
automationBlueprint.output: A Slack post in #newsroom at 5:30am, plus a dated brief file in the repo (briefs/2026-07-13.md) that builds into a searchable archive of what the routine saw.
automationBlueprint.humanCheckpoint: The news director triages the brief before any of it becomes assignment. Nothing in it is reporting, nothing in it is verified, and no item leaves the newsroom until a human has opened the primary document. The routine posts only to an internal channel — never to a public account, never to the website.
automationBlueprint.estSetupTime: A focused half-day to build and test it by hand, plus about two weeks of tuning (the first week's briefs will be too long, will miss the sources you care about most, and will over-flag routine agenda items). Budget the tuning — that's where it becomes actually useful. Do not schedule it until you've hit 'run now' several times and liked what came back.

tryThis.title: Build the brief by hand before you automate it
tryThis.setup: You need Claude Code open and the actual URLs for your city council agenda page, your county board page, and two or three local news sites. Don't schedule anything yet — the video is clear that you test a routine repeatedly before you let it fire unattended.
tryThis.prompt:
I want to build a morning story-lead brief for our newsroom, but today I just want to see what one run produces so I can judge whether it's worth automating.

Here are the sources:
- City council agendas: [paste URL]
- County board agendas: [paste URL]
- Local outlets: [paste 2-3 URLs]

Go look at what's actually there right now and write me a brief with:
1. Agenda items coming up in the next 10 days that a local reporter might care about — with a link to the actual agenda item, not a summary of it.
2. Anything in the local press from the last 24 hours that looks like it has a second-day story in it.
3. For each item, one line on why it might matter. If a category has nothing worth flagging, say so — do not pad it.

Rules: link to primary sources only. If you can't reach a source, tell me it failed instead of guessing what's on it. Do not characterize anything as fact that you haven't actually read.

After you write it, tell me honestly: which of these sources were actually machine-readable, and which would break if this ran unattended at 5:30am?

pitfalls: A routine can't see your computer. No local files, no saved logins, no browser cookies. If it has to log into something to work, it needs a real API key — the video's browser automation broke for exactly this reason.
It will not stop and ask you a question. If your prompt is ambiguous, it doesn't pause — it guesses, at 5am, with nobody watching. Write the prompt as if for someone who cannot call you.
Run limits are real: roughly 5 cloud routine runs a day on Pro, 15 on Max. One daily brief is fine. A newsroom full of hourly scanners is not.
Never point a routine at anything containing donor PII, and never let one post to a public-facing channel. Internal Slack, internal doc, internal email — that's the whole allowed list.
Don't reach for AI when a script would do. A form submission that needs to become a Slack message needs no AI at all — adding it just adds cost and a way to be wrong.

keyQuotes.quote: Think of a scheduled task or a routine as you basically typing in a prompt, and then someone coming into your laptop and typing it in for you.
Boring is beautiful. Predictability is beautiful when it comes to self-firing automations.
Build the simplest solution that you actually need for the problem. All AI would do is add a little bit of risk and increase our cost.

modules.title: What This Actually Costs — Tokens, Caching, and Session Limits
modules.kicker: The answer when your GM asks the budget question
modules.plainSummary: Claude charges you by the token — roughly one token per word — and every time you send a message it rereads the whole conversation from the beginning. That means cost compounds instead of adding: message one might be 500 tokens, message thirty might be 15,500. Prompt caching is what stops that from bankrupting you: Claude stores the unchanged part of your session and re-reads it at 10% of normal input cost. This module teaches what gets cached (system instructions, your CLAUDE.md, the conversation so far), how long the cache lives, and — most usefully — the everyday moves that silently destroy it and make your next message cost full freight.
modules.whyItMatters: Public media runs on thin margins, and somebody in leadership will ask what an AI tool costs the station before anyone is allowed to use it. The honest answer is that the plan price is fixed but the value you get out of it swings enormously with habits — one person who understands caching gets several times the work out of the same subscription as someone who doesn't. As the video puts it: most people don't need a bigger plan, they have a context hygiene problem.

concepts.plain: The smallest unit of text an AI model reads and charges you for. Roughly one token per word — not exactly, but close enough to budget with.
concepts.stationTranslation: Think of tokens as the meter on a phone line. Everything you say and everything Claude says back runs through it, and the meter never stops mid-session.

concepts.plain: Every time you send a message, Claude rereads the entire conversation from the start. So costs multiply as a chat grows — they don't just add up.
concepts.stationTranslation: Like re-reading the full show rundown out loud before every single break. Thirty breaks in, you're spending most of your air time on the rundown, not the show.

concepts.plain: Claude saves the unchanged front part of your session and reuses it. Cached tokens cost about 10% of normal input, so a long session stays affordable.
concepts.stationTranslation: The prep folder stays on the desk between segments. You don't rebuild it every hour — you just add today's new pages to it.

concepts.plain: Cache create is writing something into the cache the first time — a one-time cost that pays off next turn. Cache read is reusing it, roughly ten times cheaper than fresh input.
concepts.stationTranslation: Cache create is filing the prep folder once. Cache read is pulling the same folder off the shelf tomorrow instead of researching it all over again.

concepts.plain: How long the cache survives before it expires. One hour on a Claude subscription inside Claude Code. Five minutes by default on the API and for sub-agents.
concepts.stationTranslation: The prep folder has a shelf life. Walk away long enough and somebody clears the desk — you rebuild from scratch, at full price.

concepts.plain: System layer (base instructions, tool definitions, output style), project layer (CLAUDE.md, memory, rules), and conversation layer (your messages and Claude's replies, growing each turn).
concepts.stationTranslation: Station-wide policy, this show's format sheet, and today's script. The first two should sit still; only the script should be growing.

concepts.plain: Instead of /compact, have Claude write a summary of what you've done, the files, the open decisions, and where to pick up. Then /clear, paste the summary back, and keep going.
concepts.stationTranslation: The handoff note a morning host leaves for the afternoon host. Short, complete, and cheaper than making the next person listen to the whole shift.

glossary.definition: The smallest chunk of text an AI reads and bills for. Roughly one word, give or take.
Writing content into the cache for the first time. A one-time cost that pays off on the next turn.
Reusing already-cached content. Costs about 10% of normal input tokens — roughly ten times cheaper.
Time to live. How long cached content stays valid: one hour on a subscription, five minutes on the API.
How the cache works: it reuses content only if the front of the request is identical to last time.
A command showing exactly what is eating your tokens right now — history, MCP overhead, loaded files.
A command showing actual token usage and estimated spend for the current session.
8am-2pm Eastern on weekdays, when the five-hour session window drains faster because demand is high.

useCases.title: Answering the board's 'what does AI cost us' question with real numbers
useCases.scenario: Your GM is being asked by the board what an AI subscription buys, and whether staff will blow through it in a week.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Run /cost and /context in a real working session and screenshot both. You can show exactly where tokens go — system overhead, CLAUDE.md, conversation history — and demonstrate that cache reads run at 10% of normal input, so a disciplined user gets many times the work per dollar. The pitch becomes a habits story, not a licensing story.
useCases.timeSaved: A vague 'we think it's worth it' becomes a one-page cost briefing in about an hour
useCases.guardrail: Do not screenshot a session that has station financials, donor data, or unaired reporting on screen. Use a scratch session built for the demo.
useCases.moduleTitle: What This Actually Costs — Tokens, Caching, and Session Limits

steps: Open a fresh scratch session in Claude Code — one that never had financials, donor data, or unaired reporting anywhere on screen.
In that session, run /context and read what it shows — how many tokens you're already spending before typing a word, broken down by system, tools, and memory.
Run /cost to see actual token usage and estimated spend for the session so far.
Do a few minutes of ordinary back-and-forth work, then run /cost again to see what a cache read costs compared to a fresh message.
Take screenshots of both /cost and /context, from this scratch session only.
Turn the screenshots into a one-page briefing: what a typical session costs, and how cache reads — about 10% the price of a fresh read — change the total.
Frame the recommendation for your GM as a habits story, not a licensing story: a disciplined user gets far more work per dollar than someone who isn't.
runbook.verify: Before sending the screenshots anywhere, look at every line on screen for a name, dollar figure, or story detail that shouldn't leave the building. If you see any, the screenshot came from the wrong session — start over in a clean one.

useCases.title: A long investigative session that doesn't burn the whole day's budget
useCases.scenario: A reporter is working through a two-hundred-page records dump across a full afternoon, and the session keeps growing.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Stay in one session so the cache keeps paying off, and don't switch models mid-stream — a /model switch re-reads the entire history with no cache hits. At around 60% context, do a session handoff: ask Claude to summarize the findings, files, and open questions, then /clear and paste that summary back to continue clean.
useCases.timeSaved: A session that would hit the limit by 2pm now runs until end of day
useCases.guardrail: Confidential source material and anything under embargo does not go into a cloud tool at all. Caching is about cost, not about what is safe to upload — that judgment is separate and comes first.
useCases.moduleTitle: What This Actually Costs — Tokens, Caching, and Session Limits

steps: Before opening anything in Claude Code, decide what's actually safe to upload — confidential source material and anything under embargo does not go into a cloud tool at all, full stop.
Work the cleared records in one continuous session rather than restarting — the cache only pays off if you stay in it.
Pick one model for the whole session and don't switch mid-stream — switching models re-reads the entire conversation from the beginning with no savings.
Keep working through the afternoon, batching related questions into one message instead of scattering them across many.
Around 60% of the way through the session's context, ask Claude to summarize the findings so far, the files touched, and the open questions.
Run /clear, then paste that summary back in to keep going in a small, cheap, fresh context.
Do the same handoff any time you're stepping away for a long stretch, so you come back to a small context instead of a stale one that re-reads everything at full price.
runbook.verify: Read the handoff summary before you /clear — every finding, file, and open question from the session so far needs to be in it. If something's missing, the next stretch of work starts blind, not just cheap.

useCases.title: Pledge-drive scripting during off-peak hours
useCases.scenario: Drive week. The membership team needs dozens of script variants, and everyone is working at once on the same subscription window.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Schedule the heavy generation work for afternoons, evenings, or weekends — the five-hour session window drains faster during peak hours (8am-2pm Eastern, weekdays). Batch related asks into one message instead of three, and keep the drive's tone and rules in a lean CLAUDE.md so they're cached instead of retyped.
useCases.timeSaved: Three separate prompt rounds collapse into one; the session lasts noticeably longer
useCases.guardrail: No donor names, giving history, or CRM exports in the prompt. Aggregate, anonymized framing only — 'sustainers who gave under $10/month', never a list.
useCases.moduleTitle: What This Actually Costs — Tokens, Caching, and Session Limits

steps: Before writing a single prompt, set the rule for the whole drive: no donor names, giving history, or CRM exports ever go in — describe audiences only in aggregate terms, like 'sustainers who gave under $10 a month'.
Put the drive's tone, rules, and that data rule into a short CLAUDE.md file so it's cached and read automatically instead of retyped every time.
Batch your requests — ask for all the script variants you need in one message instead of three separate rounds.
Schedule the heavy generation sessions for afternoons, evenings, or weekends — the five-hour session window drains faster during weekday peak hours, 8am to 2pm Eastern.
Stay in one session for the whole batch instead of starting fresh each time, so the cache keeps paying off.
Have a person review every script variant against the data rule before it airs, confirming no real donor detail slipped in.
runbook.verify: Search every generated script for a real name, dollar amount, or specific giving detail. If anything beyond an aggregate description shows up, that script gets rewritten before it airs.

useCases.title: Stopping the invisible MCP token drip
useCases.scenario: Your digital lead connected four MCP servers months ago, and every session now starts thousands of tokens in the hole before anyone types a word.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Run /context in a brand-new session to see the starting overhead — the video's own fresh session began 51,000 tokens down from system prompts, tools, agents, skills, and memory. Then run /mcp and disconnect what you're not using this session. A single server can cost around 18,000 tokens on every message.
useCases.timeSaved: Recovering tens of thousands of tokens per session, every session
useCases.guardrail: Before disconnecting anything shared, check who else on staff relies on that connection. Note what you turned off so it can be turned back on.
useCases.moduleTitle: What This Actually Costs — Tokens, Caching, and Session Limits

steps: Open a brand-new Claude Code session and run /context before typing anything else, to see your starting overhead — system prompt, tools, agents, skills, and memory all cost tokens before you've said a word.
Run /mcp to see every MCP server currently connected to this session.
For each one, check who else on staff actually relies on it before touching anything — a shared connection someone else needs isn't yours to cut alone.
Disconnect the servers you don't need for this session — a single server can cost around 18,000 tokens on every message you send.
Run /context again and compare the new number to what you started with.
Write down what you turned off and why, so it can be turned back on the moment someone needs it.
runbook.verify: Compare the two /context readings — the number should drop noticeably after disconnecting. If it barely moves, the servers you disconnected weren't the ones costing you.

useCases.title: Working the CPB Local Content and Service Report without model-hopping
useCases.scenario: Someone is assembling the annual LCSR narrative across many sessions and keeps flipping between models to 'save money'.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Explain that each model has its own cache, so switching models mid-session re-reads the whole conversation from scratch. That includes the 'opus plan' setting, which uses Opus for planning and Sonnet for execution — every toggle is a model switch and starts a fresh cache. Pick a model for the session and stay in it; edit CLAUDE.md instead, which does not break the cache because it only applies on restart.
useCases.timeSaved: Avoids repeatedly paying full price to re-read a 100k-token report draft
useCases.guardrail: AI drafts the narrative; a human verifies every number against the actual station records before anything is filed with CPB.
useCases.moduleTitle: What This Actually Costs — Tokens, Caching, and Session Limits

steps: Pick one model for the whole LCSR session before you start writing, and leave 'opus plan' mode off — every model switch, including that toggle, re-reads the entire conversation from scratch with no cache savings.
Draft the narrative in that one session, working section by section rather than restarting for each part.
If you need to change instructions or tone partway through, edit CLAUDE.md instead of switching models — that edit doesn't break the cache, it just takes effect on your next session.
Keep the growing draft in the same session as long as it stays workable, so the cache keeps paying off on a long document.
Once the draft narrative is complete, go through it line by line against your actual station records — every number needs a human to verify it.
File with CPB only after that verification is done.
runbook.verify: Pick five numbers at random from the finished narrative and trace each one back to the actual station record it came from. If even one doesn't match, the draft isn't ready to file.

tryThis.title: Find out what your sessions actually cost — in ten minutes
tryThis.setup: Open Claude Code in a project you actually use. Do this in a fresh session, before you type anything else. You'll run two built-in commands first, then ask Claude to interpret them.
tryThis.prompt:
I want to understand my token costs before my GM asks me about them.

First, walk me through what /context is showing right now in this fresh session — I want to know how many tokens I'm already spending before I've said a word, and what each category (system prompt, tools, skills, memory, MCP servers) is costing me.

Then tell me, based on what you see:
1. Which MCP servers I could safely disconnect for this session, and roughly what that saves per message.
2. Whether my CLAUDE.md is lean enough — it gets re-read on every message, so flag anything over 200 lines or anything that should be an index pointer to another file instead of inline text.
3. The three habits that would most extend my session, given how I actually work.

Be concrete and give me numbers, not general advice. I'm going to put this in a memo.

pitfalls: Switching models mid-session — including turning on 'opus plan' mode — re-reads your entire conversation with zero cache hits. It feels like a savings move and it isn't.

Stepping away too long. On a subscription the cache lives an hour; on the API and for sub-agents it's five minutes by default. Come back late and your next message reprocesses everything at full cost.

Sub-agent workflows use roughly seven to ten times more tokens than a single-agent session, because each one wakes up with its own full context. Use them deliberately, and prefer Haiku for the grunt work.
Shell command output lands in your context whole. A command that returns 200 commits or a giant log dump is invisible tokens — deny permissions for commands a project genuinely doesn't need.

None of this token discipline is a privacy control. Being efficient with donor data in the prompt is not the same as being allowed to put donor data in the prompt. Decide what may be uploaded first, then optimize cost.

keyQuotes.quote: A token is the smallest unit of text that an AI model reads and charges you for. Every time you send a message, Claude rereads the entire conversation from the beginning.
Most people don't need a bigger plan. They need to stop resending their entire conversation history 30 times. It's not a limits problem, it's a context hygiene problem.
The opus plan model setting resolves to Opus during plan mode and Sonnet during execution. So each plan toggle is a model switch and starts a fresh cache.

cacheBreakers.why: Each model has its own cache. The cache works by prefix matching, and switching models changes the prefix — so the next request reads the entire conversation history with no cache hits, even though the context is identical.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: Pick a model at the start of a session and stay in it. Sonnet for most work, Opus only when Sonnet wasn't enough, and start a new session if you truly need to switch.

cacheBreakers.why: The opus plan setting resolves to Opus during plan mode and Sonnet during execution. Every plan toggle is therefore a model switch, and each switch starts a fresh cache. The video explicitly walks this back as a token-saving tip.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: Know the trade-off. It may still save session limit over the long run, but it does reset the cache each time it flips. Don't use it expecting cache savings.

cacheBreakers.why: Compacting rewrites the conversation, so the cached prefix no longer matches. The video says plainly that /compact will break the cache — and after three or four compacts in a row, output quality degrades anyway.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: Use a session handoff instead: have Claude summarize the work, files, and open decisions, then /clear and paste the summary back. Faster than /compact and it gives you a clean, cheap cache.

cacheBreakers.why: You are deliberately throwing away the conversation, so of course the cache goes with it. The next message pays cache-create prices for the new, much smaller context.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: Don't avoid it — /clear between unrelated tasks is the single best habit for extending your session. The new cache is cheap precisely because the context is small.

cacheBreakers.why: The cache expires. One hour on a Claude subscription in Claude Code, five minutes by default on the API and for sub-agents. Leave a session sitting and your next message reprocesses everything from scratch at full cost. This is why people see usage spike out of nowhere after a break.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: If you're stepping away for a long stretch, do a session handoff or /clear before you go, so you come back to a small context rather than an expensive re-read of a huge one.

cacheBreakers.why: The system layer sits at the very front of the cached prefix. Change it on message 16 and everything from the beginning must be reprocessed — an expensive move made late in a long session.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: Settle your system-level setup (instructions, tool definitions, output style) before the session starts, not halfway through it.

cacheBreakers.why: A sub-agent is a separate instance that wakes up with its own full context and reloads everything. Agent workflows use roughly seven to ten times the tokens of a standard single-agent session, and sub-agent caches carry a five-minute TTL on any plan.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: Use sub-agents deliberately, for one-off research or bulk processing where only a summary comes back — and run them on Haiku so the expensive tokens stay in your main session.

cacheBreakers.why: This is the one that surprises people. The edit doesn't apply until you restart the session, so the cached project layer stays exactly as it was. The cache stays safe.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: Nothing to avoid — go ahead and edit. Just remember your changes take effect on the next session, not this one.

cacheBreakers.action: Sending your next message within the TTL
cacheBreakers.why: This is the cache doing its job. The system layer, project layer, and everything said so far are already cached — Claude only has to newly process the last reply and your new message, then cache those too.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: Nothing to avoid. Keep the session alive, keep it focused, and let the cache reads pile up at 10% of normal input cost.

cacheBreakers.action: Blowing past your weekly limit into pay-per-token usage
cacheBreakers.why: It doesn't break the cache directly, but it quietly changes the rules: you leave subscription territory and land on API pricing, where the default TTL drops from an hour to five minutes. Sessions that felt fine now re-cache constantly.
cacheBreakers.avoidBy: Watch your usage dashboard. If you spill into extra usage, tighten up — shorter gaps between messages, or bump the TTL back to an hour at additional cost.

costModel.notes: Numbers as stated in the transcript. Cached tokens cost 10% of normal input — the video's example: 91 million cached tokens in a day billed like about 9 million. TTL on a Claude subscription inside Claude Code is 1 hour; on the API it is 5 minutes by default, and it can be bumped to 1 hour at extra cost. Sub-agents get 5 minutes on any plan. claude.ai web projects are NOT clearly documented — the video's presenter assumes they behave like the subscription but says he could not confirm it, and only that dropping documents into a project appears better optimized than pasting them into chat. Other figures worth modeling: a single MCP server can add ~18,000 tokens per message; a fresh session in the video already started ~51,000 tokens down from system prompt, tools, agents, skills, and memory; sub-agent workflows run roughly 7-10x the tokens of a single-agent session; autocompact triggers around 95% context, but manual compaction is recommended around 60%. Cost compounds rather than adds: the video's illustration has message 1 at ~500 tokens and message 30 at ~15,500, reaching nearly a quarter-million cumulative tokens after 30 messages, with one tracked 100+ message chat spending 98.5% of its tokens rereading old history.

glossary.definition: File holding secrets like API keys. Kept local and excluded from anything you publish or share.
A list of files GitHub should never upload — API keys, passwords, anything sensitive. Check it before your first push.
Wipes the session clean. Fresh context, zero memory of what you just did — so hand off first.
Claude Code command that summarizes the conversation so far and keeps going. Runs automatically when you near the limit — often too late.
A command showing exactly what is eating your tokens right now — history, MCP overhead, loaded files.
A command showing actual token usage and estimated spend for the current session.
AI that reasons and decides in a loop rather than following fixed steps. Powerful, variable, more expensive, fails in surprising ways.
Several agents that share a task list and can talk to each other, debate, and reach consensus. More expensive than sub-agents. Experimental feature.
A password letting one tool pull data from another. Anyone holding it can use your account.
A Claude Code setting where it writes and updates a memory file on its own as you work. Toggle it with /memory.
A link from one note to another. Follow it to jump from 'spring drive 2024' to 'sustainer messaging.'
A folder with your logo and brand guidelines that you point Claude at, so what it builds comes out in your colors and type.
Mode where Claude Code never asks for approval. Also called 'dangerously skip permissions.' It means it.
Writing content into the cache for the first time. A one-time cost that pays off on the next turn.
Reusing already-cached content. Costs about 10% of normal input tokens — roughly ten times cheaper.
Plain-English instructions file Claude Code reads before every message. Your standing rules and context.
Command-line interface. A tool you operate by typing commands instead of clicking. Free, fast, and easy for Claude to drive.
A prebuilt link between Claude and a tool you already use — Slack, Gmail, Google Drive. You switch it on in settings.
Everything the AI actually knows about your situation: your files, your history, your priorities. Not the same as your prompt.
Filling the AI's working memory with exactly the right information. Karpathy's phrase. The durable skill.
The quality drop that happens as a session's context window fills up. Also called 'the dumb zone.'
How much the AI can hold in its head at once — like a producer's prep folder for one show. It is finite.
The old term for 'run this on a clock.' You don't need to learn the syntax; you can just say 'weekdays at 6am.'
Moving your work from a private preview on your own computer to a public address the world can open.
Claude fans a big job out to many sub-agents at once — sometimes dozens. Powerful, but it eats your usage limit fast.
A password or API key handed to a program at startup instead of written into its files. Keeps secrets out of shared code.
A set of known inputs with known correct outputs. Run them against your automation to see how often it gets it right before you trust it.
The settings block at the top of a sub-agent's markdown file: name, description, allowed tools, model, memory. Everything below it is the instructions.
A cloud home for your project's files that keeps every past version, so any change can be traced or undone.
Claude's models: fast-and-cheap, balanced, and most-capable-and-priciest. Same tool, different horsepower.
The tool wrapped around an AI model that gives it hands — file access, web search, memory. Claude Code is a harness.
A table of contents the AI reads first, so it knows which note to open instead of reading everything.
What you feed the model vs what it writes back. Output costs about 5x more per token.
Storage that records how things relate — 'Jordan works at Acme,' 'Acme sponsors Morning Edition' — not just that they're linked.
Periodically asking the AI to audit its own notes for gaps, contradictions, and missing information.
A private preview of your site that runs only on your own machine. Nobody else can see it.
Plain text with light formatting. Opens in any editor, readable in 20 years, needs no software to survive.
Model Context Protocol. A standard way to plug Claude Code into another tool, like Gmail or QuickBooks.
The index file that points to those individual memory files. One fact per file; MEMORY.md is the table of contents.
A sub-agent fires when you didn't want it, or stays silent when you did. Fixed by rewriting its description, not its instructions.
Cheap hosting services that run a small script on a schedule or on a webhook. Pennies per run. Used in the video's build.
The raw AI brain — Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, Fable. Interchangeable. Claude Code runs on the same ones as Claude Chat.
The 'do NOT do this' part of your instructions. Guardrails, stated before the work starts.
The one number you pick before you build, that tells you afterward whether the build was worth it.
The 'Sign in with Google, allow access' step. How you grant Claude reach into your own Workspace account, revocably.
A free app that displays your markdown files and draws the links between them as a picture. Optional — the files work without it.
Your main session. The only one you talk to. It hands work out to sub-agents and relays their findings back to you.
8am-2pm Eastern on weekdays, when the five-hour session window drains faster because demand is high.
Setting controlling how often Claude Code stops to ask before acting. Auto is the default.
How the cache works: it reuses content only if the front of the request is identical to last time.
Claude skims every Skill's description, then fully reads only the one it needs. Keeps forty Skills from crowding out your actual work.
Wording a request well. Was a big deal; matters less each year as models improve on their own.
One project's folder on GitHub — all the files plus the full history of who changed what and when.
A saved prompt Claude Code runs by itself on a schedule, on Anthropic's servers, with your machine off.
The record of every automated run. When one fails, you paste the failed run back to Claude and ask it why.
Same idea, but it runs on your own computer — so your machine has to be on and awake.
An API key restricted to specific capabilities and spending limits. Read-only, or one narrow function.
Finding notes by meaning rather than exact wording. Searching 'listener complaints' surfaces a note that says 'audience feedback.'
On a Claude subscription you get a rolling 5-hour usage window plus a weekly cap, instead of per-token billing.
Config file in the .claude folder listing what Claude Code is allowed and denied from doing.
A folder with a SKILL.md file describing one procedure. Claude loads it on its own when the request matches.
A separate AI worker with a fresh, empty memory, given one job by your main session. Reports back a summary. Defined in one markdown file.
Work only compounds at the bottleneck. Unclog the biggest one, and the next one becomes the biggest.
The smallest chunk of text an AI reads and bills for. Roughly one word, give or take.
Set up so your work survives switching AI apps. Files, folders, and your own keys move; app-specific connectors don't.
Time to live. How long cached content stays valid: one hour on a subscription, five minutes on the API.
A service that takes the files in your GitHub repo and publishes them as a live website at a real web address.
Making the AI check its own output against something real before it hands the work back to you.
A doorbell. One system pings another the instant something happens, instead of the second system constantly checking.
A fixed if-this-then-that sequence with no AI in it. Predictable, cheap, boring, almost never breaks. Often the right answer.
The few labeled lines at the top of a SKILL.md — name, description, when to use it. Claude reads these to decide.

useCases.title: The news director who has never opened a terminal
useCases.scenario: You get a 400-page county budget PDF the night before a council vote and one reporter who is already filing two other stories.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Drop the PDF in a folder, point Claude Code at it, and ask it to pull every line item that changed by more than 10% year over year, with page numbers. It reads the whole thing in minutes and cites where it found each figure so your reporter can verify against the source. This is the local-files idea from Chapter 1 — the AI works in your folder, not in a browser tab.
useCases.timeSaved: A full evening of skimming becomes ~20 minutes of reading a cited summary
useCases.guardrail: Nothing it produces goes on air or online until your reporter has opened the actual PDF page it cited and confirmed the number. AI finds the lead; a human confirms it. Always.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Save the budget PDF into a folder on your computer — call it something like "budget-story" so you can find it again.
Open Claude Code and point it at that folder (this just means telling it which folder on your computer to work in).
Ask it to read the whole PDF and list every line item where the amount changed by more than 10% since last year.
Ask it to give you the page number in the PDF where it found each one, not just the number itself.
Open the actual PDF to each page it cited and check that the number really says what Claude said it says.
Hand your reporter only the items you've personally confirmed on the page, with the page numbers attached.
runbook.verify: Open the PDF to the page number Claude cited for each flagged line item — if what's on that page doesn't match the number in Claude's list, pull that item before it goes anywhere near a script.

useCases.title: Onboard Claude to your drive the way you'd onboard an intern
useCases.scenario: Every fall drive, you rewrite the same pitch scripts from scratch because last year's live in someone's Google Drive and nobody remembers which ones actually converted.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Put your past scripts, your goal sheet, and your notes on what worked and what flopped into one folder — the summer-intern move from Chapter 4. Now Claude Code drafts against your station's actual voice and history instead of generic best practices. Correct it once, tell it why, and the next draft is closer.
useCases.timeSaved: Two days of script rewriting becomes an afternoon of editing
useCases.guardrail: Scripts and copy only. Never put the donor file in that folder — no names, no addresses, no giving history, no CRM export. The AI needs your language, not your list.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Gather only past pitch scripts, your goal sheet, and notes on what worked or flopped — leave out anything with a donor's name, email, address, or CRM export.
Put those files in one folder on your computer, named something like "drive-context."
Open Claude Code and point it at that folder.
Ask it to read everything and tell you back, in its own words, what your station's pitch voice sounds like and what's worked before.
Ask it to draft this year's pitch scripts in that voice, aimed at the goals on your goal sheet.
Read every draft, mark up what's off, and tell Claude Code why — not just what — you changed it.
Ask for one more pass using your corrections before you call any script final.
runbook.verify: Open the drive-context folder yourself and search it for anything that looks like a donor name, email, or CRM export — if you find one, delete it and start the folder over before Claude Code ever reads it.

useCases.title: Know when the answer is 'this needs no AI at all'
useCases.scenario: Your GM wants 'an AI' to handle underwriting spot copy end to end because a vendor demo looked impressive.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Use the vending-machine-vs-slot-machine test from Chapter 5. Emailing the weekly spot schedule is a vending machine — a plain scheduled script, no AI, never fails. Turning a client's messy intake email into a compliant draft spot is genuinely messy input and needs reasoning, so that part earns the AI. Being the person who can draw that line is the point.
useCases.timeSaved: Avoids a six-week build for something a five-minute script does
useCases.guardrail: FCC rules do not care that a machine wrote it. Every spot still gets human compliance review: no calls to action, no price claims, no qualitative claims. That review never gets automated.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Write down every underwriting task your GM wants "an AI" to handle, one per line.
For each one, ask: does this follow the exact same steps every single time, or does it need someone to read something messy and make a judgment call?
Route the same-every-time tasks — like emailing the weekly spot schedule — to a plain scheduled script. That's not a Claude Code job at all.
For the genuinely messy task — turning a client's rambling intake email into a spot draft — open Claude Code and ask it to draft compliant copy from that email, in your station's usual format.
Tell it plainly, up front, what a spot must never contain: no calls to action, no price claims, no qualitative claims.
Send every draft it produces through your normal FCC compliance review before it reaches a client or the air.
Show your GM the actual split — one boring script, one Claude Code draft with human review — as the real answer to "get an AI on this."
runbook.verify: Read the compliance reviewer's notes on the first three Claude-drafted spots — if a call-to-action or price claim slipped through in any of them, tighten your instructions before you trust the next batch.

useCases.title: Prep the local-music segment without the tab-switching
useCases.scenario: Your local music host spends two hours before every show pulling artist bios, tour dates, and release news across a dozen browser tabs.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: This is the 'what your hand reaches for' shift. Instead of opening the same tabs, hand Claude Code the week's playlist file and let it web-search and assemble a single prep sheet per artist. It reads your local file and reaches out online — both at once, which Chat cannot do.
useCases.timeSaved: A 2-hour prep block becomes ~25 minutes of review
useCases.guardrail: Anything the host says on air about an artist — a tour date, a label, a hometown — gets confirmed against a real source. A hallucinated tour date is your station's error, not the AI's.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Open your week's playlist file — the one your local-music host already has.
Put it in a folder on your computer and open Claude Code there.
Ask Claude Code to read the playlist and, for each local artist, search online for a short bio, upcoming tour dates, and any recent release news.
Ask it to put all of that into one prep sheet, organized by artist, in the order they're playing.
Read the prep sheet and flag anything that sounds off or too good to be true.
Before your host says anything on air about a tour date, label, or hometown, check it against a real source — the artist's own site or a wire story.
runbook.verify: Pick three claims from the prep sheet — a tour date, a label, a hometown — and look each one up yourself on the artist's own site before your host reads any of them on air.

useCases.title: Budget for the dip before you report on it
useCases.scenario: You green-light an AI pilot and someone asks for results after two weeks. The team is slower than before and morale is dropping.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: That is the dip from Chapter 6, and it is expected, not failure — a short-term productivity cost before the exponential return. Set the definition-of-done up front: one team, one workflow, one metric (hours off show prep, days off the LCSR draft), and a realistic window. Then judge it.
useCases.timeSaved: Prevents killing a working pilot three weeks early
useCases.guardrail: Disclose to your board and to staff what you are piloting and where. Public media runs on trust; a quiet AI rollout that surfaces later is a much bigger problem than a slow one announced early.
useCases.moduleTitle: Orientation: What Claude Code Actually Is

steps: Before you announce the pilot, pick exactly one team, one workflow, and one number you're measuring — hours off show prep, days off the LCSR draft, whatever's real for that team.
Set a realistic window for judging it — a few weeks, not a few days — and write that window down where the team can see it.
Tell your board and your staff what you're piloting and where, before anyone asks. Public trust runs on this, not surprise announcements.
When week one comes in slower than the old process, name it out loud as "the dip," not a verdict on the pilot.
Keep a simple running log of the one number you picked, every week of the window.
At the end of the window — not before — sit down with the team and the log, and decide, based on that number, whether it worked.
runbook.verify: Look at the log: compare the number from week one to the number from the final week. If you can't put two real numbers side by side, you don't have a verdict — you have a feeling, and a feeling is what would have killed this pilot three weeks early.

useCases.title: Turn the pledge-drive export into a board-ready readout
useCases.scenario: The drive ended Sunday. You have a 3,000-row export from the CRM and a board meeting Thursday, and nobody has time to build the pivot tables again.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Strip the export down to non-identifying columns first — gift amount, date, hour, premium, new-vs-renewing, channel — and save that anonymized file locally. Point Claude Code at it and ask for a workbook: dashboard tab, hour-by-hour take, new vs. renewing split, comparison to last drive. Add the verification line so it re-sums every column against the source before handing it over.
useCases.timeSaved: A two-day spreadsheet weekend becomes about 30 minutes plus a review pass
useCases.guardrail: Donor names, emails, addresses, phone numbers and giving history never leave your CRM. Export the numbers, not the people. Strip PII before Claude ever sees the file.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Export only the columns you need from your CRM — gift amount, date, hour, premium, new-vs-renewing, channel. No names, emails, addresses, or phone numbers.
Put that stripped file in a folder on your computer called something like "drive-readout."
Open Claude Code in that folder and ask it to read the file and list back the columns it sees, before it does anything else.
Ask for the workbook you need: a summary tab, an hour-by-hour take, a new-vs-renewing split, and a comparison to your last drive.
Tell it to re-sum every column against the source file and show you that check before handing the workbook back.
Check the workbook's totals against your CRM's own totals for the drive before the numbers go anywhere near the board.
Save the prompt as a Skill, so the station owns this for next drive, not just you.
runbook.verify: Open the finished workbook and search it for an @ sign — if any email address survived the strip, it isn't safe to work with; start over from a clean export.

useCases.title: Read the music log without reading the music log
useCases.scenario: Your automation system spits out a play log every week. You want to know whether local artist rotation actually hit the target you promised in the last grant, and whether new music share is drifting.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Drop the log export in a folder and ask Claude Code to tally spins by artist, flag which are tagged local, chart the local percentage week over week, and write the counts into a spreadsheet you can reuse. Tell it explicitly not to guess an artist's local status when the tag is blank — flag those rows for a human instead.
useCases.timeSaved: A 4-hour manual tally becomes ~20 minutes
useCases.guardrail: Claude counts spins; it does not decide licensing or reporting obligations. Anything headed to SoundExchange, ASCAP or BMI goes through the person who actually owns that filing.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Export this week's play log from your automation system.
Put the file in a folder on your computer and open Claude Code there.
Ask it to read the log, tally spins by artist, and flag which ones are tagged local.
Tell it plainly: if the local tag is blank, don't guess — flag that row for you to decide, don't count it either way.
Ask it to chart the local percentage week over week and write the counts into a spreadsheet you can reuse.
Review the flagged blank-tag rows yourself and fill in the local status by hand.
Compare the resulting local percentage to the number you promised in your last grant, before you report it anywhere.
runbook.verify: Pick five rows at random from the tally and count their spins yourself straight from the log — if your hand count doesn't match what Claude reported for those five, don't trust the rest of the tally either.

useCases.title: Assemble the CPB Local Content & Service Report evidence pile
useCases.scenario: The LCSR is due and the underlying numbers are scattered across five spreadsheets, three departments and one person's Downloads folder.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Ask Claude Code to find the relevant files across your project folder, read them, and build a single consolidated worksheet with every figure traced back to its source file and cell. Then ask it to prove the work: recompute each total from the raw file and show its arithmetic next to the number it reported.
useCases.timeSaved: A 6-hour hunt-and-reconcile pass becomes ~45 minutes
useCases.guardrail: Claude assembles and cross-checks; a human signs the report. Never let it fill a gap by estimating — an empty cell stays empty and gets escalated.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Gather the five spreadsheets — wherever they actually live, including that one person's Downloads folder — into a single folder on your computer.
Open Claude Code and point it at that folder.
Ask it to find every figure the LCSR needs across those files and build one worksheet, with each number traced back to its source file and cell.
Tell it plainly: if a number isn't in any of the files, leave the cell empty and flag it for you — never estimate or guess a fill-in.
Ask it to recompute each total straight from the raw file and show its arithmetic next to the number it reported.
Chase down the real number yourself for every flagged empty cell before the report goes anywhere.
Have the person who owns the LCSR filing review and sign the consolidated worksheet before it's submitted.
runbook.verify: Pick three numbers on the consolidated worksheet, open the source file and cell it cites, and check the number by eye — if any of the three don't match their cited cell, the whole worksheet needs a re-check before anyone signs it.

useCases.title: Make the podcast download CSV mean something
useCases.scenario: You get a monthly download CSV per show. Leadership asks 'how are the podcasts doing,' and you have never had a clean way to answer with a trend rather than a number.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Point Claude Code at the folder of monthly CSVs. Ask it to merge them, chart downloads per episode and per show over time, flag the episodes that outperformed their show's median, and write it into one workbook you re-run each month. Cheap, structural work — this is a Haiku or Sonnet job, not an Opus one.
useCases.timeSaved: A recurring 2-hour monthly chore becomes a 5-minute re-run
useCases.guardrail: Download platforms count differently. Ask Claude to state the definition it used for a 'download' and keep the platform's own methodology note attached to the report.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Collect the monthly download CSV for each show into one folder on your computer.
Open Claude Code and point it at that folder.
Ask it to merge the CSVs and chart downloads per episode and per show over time.
Ask it to flag which episodes outperformed their own show's median.
Ask it to state, in plain words, exactly how it's defining a "download" — and keep that definition attached to the report.
Write the result into one workbook you can point Claude Code at again next month, instead of starting over.
runbook.verify: Pick one show's total from the workbook and add up that show's downloads yourself straight from its monthly CSVs — if your total doesn't match, check whether the merge dropped a month or double-counted one.

useCases.title: Reconcile the traffic report against what actually aired
useCases.scenario: A sponsor claims their spots didn't run. You have the traffic log and the as-aired report, and comparing them by eye is how people lose afternoons.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Give Claude Code both exports and ask it to match scheduled spots against as-aired, list every discrepancy with date, time and spot ID, and produce a make-good candidate list. Tell it to show the matching rows for each discrepancy so you can spot-check the logic rather than trust it.
useCases.timeSaved: A 3-hour line-by-line reconciliation becomes ~25 minutes
useCases.guardrail: Claude finds mismatches; it does not draft the client response or write spot copy. Any copy that reaches air goes through FCC compliance review — no calls to action, no price or qualitative claims.
useCases.moduleTitle: First Contact — Point It at a Real Station File

steps: Export both the traffic log (what was scheduled) and the as-aired report (what actually ran).
Put both files in one folder on your computer and open Claude Code there.
Ask it to match scheduled spots against as-aired ones and list every discrepancy with date, time, and spot ID.
Ask it to show you the actual matching rows behind each discrepancy, not just its conclusion.
Ask it to produce a make-good candidate list from those discrepancies.
Spot-check a handful of the discrepancies yourself against the two original files before you tell the client anything.
Hand the confirmed list to whoever owns the client relationship — Claude drafts no response and no spot copy.
runbook.verify: Pick three discrepancies from the list and look up those exact date, time, and spot-ID rows in both original files yourself — if the mismatch isn't really there, the matching logic is wrong and the whole list needs a re-run.

useCases.title: A CRM key that can look but not touch
useCases.scenario: Your development director wants Claude Code to summarize lapsed-donor trends by segment, but the last thing anyone wants is an agent writing to the donor database.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Generate a read-only, scoped API key in your CRM — read access only, no edit, no delete, no export-all — and store it in .env. Then add a deny rule in settings.json so Claude Code can never run a delete or write command against it. Ask for aggregate counts, never named records.
useCases.timeSaved: A quarterly segment pull that took a week of back-and-forth with IT becomes an afternoon
useCases.guardrail: Never paste a donor export, name, address, email, or giving history into the prompt box. Aggregates only. If the key can write, it is the wrong key.
useCases.moduleTitle: Set Up Your Station's Workspace — And Its Guardrails

steps: In your CRM, create a new API key that is read-only — no edit, no delete, no export-all — and confirm in the CRM's own settings screen that it physically cannot write.
Save that key in a file called .env in your project folder, and never paste it anywhere else — not in CLAUDE.md, not in an email, not in Slack.
Open Claude Code in that folder and ask it to add a deny rule in settings.json blocking any write or delete command against the CRM.
Ask it to explain in plain English what that deny rule actually blocks, so you know it did what you asked before you rely on it.
Ask Claude for the donor segment summary you need — aggregate counts and totals only, never a request for named records.
Read the result back and confirm every line is a count or a total, with no donor name, email, or address anywhere in it.
runbook.verify: Go back to your CRM's key management screen and confirm the key shows read-only. If it shows any write or delete permission, delete the key, generate a new one, and redo the setup before using it again.

useCases.title: The newsroom style guide the AI actually follows
useCases.scenario: Every reporter re-explains the same rules to the AI: how you refer to the mayor's office, which sources need a second confirmation, that you never use 'slammed' in a lede.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Put all of it in a project CLAUDE.md once. When an output comes back wrong, tell Claude to update its own CLAUDE.md so it never repeats the mistake — the file gets smarter every time you correct it, instead of you retyping the same note.
useCases.timeSaved: Ten minutes of setup instructions per session drops to zero
useCases.guardrail: Unpublished investigative material and confidential source identities go nowhere near the prompt box. CLAUDE.md should say so explicitly, in writing, at the top.
useCases.moduleTitle: Set Up Your Station's Workspace — And Its Guardrails

steps: Open Claude Code in your newsroom project folder and ask it to create (or open) CLAUDE.md, with this as the very first rule: never accept unpublished investigative material or confidential source identities, in any form.
Tell Claude your house style — how you refer to the mayor's office, which sources need a second confirmation, banned words like 'slammed' — and have it write those rules into CLAUDE.md underneath.
Ask Claude to read the file back to you and confirm the no-go rule is still the first thing anyone sees when it opens the file.
Use Claude Code normally on a story that has no unpublished material or source names anywhere near it.
The next time an output comes back wrong — wrong style, wrong assumption — tell Claude to update CLAUDE.md itself so it doesn't repeat that mistake.
Ask it to show you exactly what changed in the file before you move on to the next story.
runbook.verify: Open CLAUDE.md and check that the unpublished-material rule is still the first rule in the file. If a later edit pushed it down or dropped it, put it back at the top before anyone uses this folder again.

useCases.title: Compliance rules baked into the workspace
useCases.scenario: An account executive drafts spot copy and keeps having to strip out calls to action and price claims that would violate FCC underwriting rules.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Write the FCC constraints directly into a project CLAUDE.md — no calls to action, no qualitative or comparative claims, no prices, no inducements to buy. Claude Code reads it before every draft, so the copy comes back closer to compliant on the first pass.
useCases.timeSaved: Three compliance rewrites per spot becomes roughly one
useCases.guardrail: A CLAUDE.md rule is a first filter, not a lawyer. Every spot still gets a human compliance review before it airs. Never treat the AI's 'this is compliant' as clearance.
useCases.moduleTitle: Set Up Your Station's Workspace — And Its Guardrails

steps: Open Claude Code in your underwriting project folder and open (or create) CLAUDE.md.
Write in the FCC constraints in plain language: no calls to action, no price or comparative claims, no inducements to buy — sponsor name and a factual description only.
Add a line requiring every draft to end with a plain statement that it hasn't been cleared and needs human compliance review.
Ask Claude to draft copy for a real or practice sponsor, then check the result against the rules you just wrote, line by line.
If anything slips past the rules, tell Claude exactly what it got wrong and have it add that specific example to CLAUDE.md.
Send every draft to your compliance reviewer before it airs, regardless of how clean Claude's version looks.
runbook.verify: Read the draft's closing note out loud. If it doesn't literally say the spot hasn't been cleared and needs a human review, the CLAUDE.md rule didn't take — fix it before the draft goes anywhere near a client.

useCases.title: Deciding what the station will and won't send to a vendor
useCases.scenario: Staff are already pasting things into AI tools and nobody has written down what is allowed. The board asks what your policy is.
useCases.howClaudeHelps: Use this module's plain framing to write one: closed models send every prompt to a vendor's servers; turning off training does not delete the data; scoped keys and settings.json deny-rules are how you enforce limits technically instead of by memo.
